01 — Fundamentals, Types & Operators

Topic Map: Object model → Types → Truthiness → Operators → LEGB scope → Namespaces → Gotchas

1. Python's Object Model

Python is dynamically typed (types checked at runtime) and strongly typed (no implicit "5" + 5). Variables are names bound to objects on the heap — not memory boxes.

1.1 Everything is an object

>>> x = 42
>>> type(x)            # <class 'int'>
>>> type(int)          # <class 'type'>
>>> type(type)         # <class 'type'>  ← metaclass of all classes
>>> id(x)              # unique identity (CPython: often memory address)
>>> isinstance(42, int)  # True

1.2 Assignment = binding a name to an object

a = [1, 2]
b = a           # same object — NOT a copy
b.append(3)     # a is now [1, 2, 3]
c = a[:]        # shallow copy — new list object

2. Built-in Types Reference

TypeMutableHashableNotes
NoneType—YesSingleton. Always use x is None.
boolNoYesSubclass of int. True==1.
intNoYesArbitrary precision. Literals: 0xFF, 0b101, 1_000.
floatNoYes*IEEE 754. Never for currency. *nan unhashable.
complexNoYes*3+4j, .real, .imag, abs(3+4j)==5.
strNoYesUnicode. Some literals interned.
bytesNoYes0–255 immutable sequence.
listYesNoDynamic array of references.
tupleNoIf elements areRecords, dict keys, multi-return.
dictYesNoHash table, ordered since 3.7.
setYesNoUnique elements, O(1) avg lookup.

3. Truthiness & Boolean Logic

3.1 Falsy values (all else truthy)

None, False, zero numbers (0, 0.0, 0j), empty sequences/collections ("", [], (), {}, set(), range(0)).

bool("")        # False
bool("False")   # True — non-empty string!
bool([0])       # True — non-empty list!

3.2 Short-circuit: and, or, not

# or:  first truthy value, or last if all falsy
# and: first falsy value, or last if all truthy
name = user or "Anonymous"
if obj and obj.valid(): ...  # safe attribute access pattern

3.3 Chained comparisons

1 < x < 10  # x evaluated ONCE (unlike: 1 < x and x < 10 in all cases)

4. Operators & Precedence

Top to bottom (high binds tighter): ** → unary +-~ → *///%@ → +- → shifts → &^| → comparisons → not → and → or → conditional → lambda → walrus.

4.1 Identity vs equality

OperatorChecksUse for
==Value via __eq__Data comparison
isSame object (id)None, sentinels only
!=Not equal
[] is []       # False — new objects each time
256 is 256      # True — small int cache (-5..256 in CPython)
257 is 257      # May be False — do NOT rely on is for ints

5. Scope — LEGB Rule

  1. Local — current function
  2. Enclosing — outer functions (closures)
  3. Global — module level
  4. Builtins — len, print, etc.
x = "global"
def outer():
    x = "enclosing"
    def inner():
        nonlocal x
        x = "inner"
    inner()
    return x  # "inner"

def trap():
    print(x)   # UnboundLocalError — x assigned below = local for whole fn
    x = 2

5.1 global vs nonlocal

count = 0
def inc_global():
    global count
    count += 1

def make_counter():
    n = 0
    def inc():
        nonlocal n
        n += 1
        return n
    return inc

6. Namespaces

globals()  # module namespace dict
locals()   # current scope dict
dir(obj)   # list attribute names

# Each module/class/instance typically has __dict__ (unless __slots__)

7. Walrus Operator (3.8+)

if (n := len(data)) > 10:
    print(f"Large dataset: {n} items")

while (line := file.readline()):
    process(line)
Critical Gotchas:
  • def f(x=[]) — mutable default evaluated once at definition, shared across calls
  • def f(x=time.time()) — frozen timestamp at definition
  • 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 — float imprecision; use decimal.Decimal
  • Never use is for value comparison of strings/ints (interning tricks you)
EAFP vs LBYL: Python culture prefers try/except (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission) over excessive precondition checks.
How to think about this topic: Imagine Python as a graph of objects on the heap and names as sticky notes pointing to those objects. Assignment never moves data — it moves the sticky note. When confused, draw boxes (objects) and arrows (names). Every bug in later topics (memory, OOP, closures) traces back to misunderstanding this picture.

Before you start Q1–Q60

  • You can explain LEGB without looking at notes.
  • You can predict output of is vs == on lists and small ints.
  • You know every falsy value by heart.

How Python runs your code (language feel)

python script.py → lexer/parser → bytecode (.pyc) → CPython VM executes opcodes. REPL compiles each snippet the same way. import m runs m.py top-to-bottom once, then caches in sys.modules.

import dis
def f(x): return x + 1
dis.dis(f)   # LOAD_FAST, LOAD_CONST, BINARY_ADD, RETURN_VALUE

This is why Python feels "interpreted" but is actually compile-then-run — unlike C++ where you link native machine code ahead of time.

Connects forward

→ Control flow uses truthiness every iteration. → Memory formalizes references. → Functions adds new scope layers. → Everything else builds on objects + names.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Names reference objects; assignment never copies unless you explicitly copy.
  2. Master falsy/truthy rules — they appear in every conditional.
  3. LEGB + global/nonlocal explain nearly all scope bugs.
  4. is for None; == for values.
  5. Mutable defaults are the #1 function-definition trap.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: none

Q1 HARD
Predict `[] is []`, `() is ()`, `256 is 256`, `257 is 257`
Your goal:

Master a core skill in fundamentals by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Analyze and predict exact output/behavior for: Predict `[] is []`, `() is ()`, `256 is 256`, `257 is 257` — explain CPython interning For each case, write the predicted result, then verify by running CPython 3.11+. Explain the underlying mechanism (interning, identity, caching, etc.) in 2–4 sentences per case.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Written explanation + verified code output match; no hand-waving.
Q2 HARD
Implement `deep_equal(a,b)` with cycle detection without recursive `==`.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in fundamentals by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement `deep_equal(a,b)` with cycle detection without recursive `==`. Start by listing inputs/outputs on paper, then edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Your solution must be runnable end-to-end, not a snippet.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — do not use high-level stdlib shortcuts unless the question explicitly allows it.
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Must handle cyclic references without infinite recursion.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.
Q3 BRUTAL

Master a core skill in fundamentals by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

Q29 HARD
Config precedence: env>CLI>file>defaults.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in fundamentals by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Config precedence: env>CLI>file>defaults. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Support command-line usage with argparse or interactive REPL loop.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Q50 BRUTAL
Mini interpreter
Your goal:

Master a core skill in fundamentals by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Mini interpreter — tokenizer+parser+eval (200+ lines). Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Provide clear error messages with line/column on parse failure.
  • Expect 150+ lines of well-structured code — plan modules before coding.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Complete Expression Language Interpreter (Phase 1)
Your goal:

Build mental stamina for long parsing/evaluation pipelines — foundation for all later capstones.

What you must do:

Design and implement a full expression language from scratch (300+ lines expected). PHASE 1 — Tokenizer: recognize NUMBER, IDENT, operators (+,-,*,/,**,%), parentheses, whitespace, and produce a token stream with line/column metadata. PHASE 2 — Recursive-descent parser: build an AST for arithmetic with correct operator precedence and unary minus. PHASE 3 — Evaluator: walk AST, return float/int, raise ParseError/SyntaxError with human-readable messages. PHASE 4 — REPL loop: read input until blank line or 'exit', evaluate, print result. PHASE 5 — Add variables: assignment `x = expr` stored in environment dict, lookup in expressions.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No eval(), exec(), ast.parse(), or third-party parsers.
  • Tokenizer, Parser, Evaluator as separate classes in separate files or clear sections.
  • Minimum 20 unit tests: precedence, unary ops, nested parens, undefined variable, bad syntax.
  • Error messages must include token position.
  • REPL must survive bad input without crashing.
Done when: REPL evaluates `x=2+3*4; x/2` correctly; all tests pass; code readable with type hints on public methods.
Q52 MEGA
Object Identity & Interning Research Lab
Your goal:

Deeply internalize CPython object identity rules that trip up seniors in interviews.

What you must do:

Build an interactive CLI tool `identity_lab.py` that empirically tests and documents CPython behavior. Implement experiments for: small int caching range, string interning (compile-time vs runtime), empty tuple singleton, list/tuple identity, `is` vs `==` on NaN floats, custom class __eq__ without __hash__. For each experiment: hypothesis → code → actual output → written explanation. Generate an HTML report summarizing all findings with code snippets.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • At least 12 distinct experiments with reproducible scripts.
  • Detect Python version and note version-specific behavior.
  • Include at least 3 'surprising' cases that violate beginner intuition.
  • HTML report auto-generated from experiment results dict.
Done when: Report readable standalone; each experiment runnable via `python identity_lab.py --experiment N`.
Q53 MEGA
Configuration System With Full Precedence Chain
Your goal:

Practice long conditional logic and namespace thinking used in real apps.

What you must do:

Implement `ConfigLoader` merging settings from 4 sources with strict precedence (highest wins): (1) environment variables prefixed `APP_`, (2) CLI flags via argparse, (3) TOML/INI file, (4) hardcoded defaults. Support nested keys like `database.host`. Support type coercion: int, float, bool (`true`/`false`/`1`/`0`), list (comma-separated). Log every resolved value with its winning source for debugging.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Single public API: `load_config(argv=None, env=None, path=None) -> Config` (MappingProxy or frozen dataclass).
  • Nested key access: `config.get('database.host')` or attribute access.
  • CLI `--config path` and `--set key=value` override file.
  • 15+ tests covering precedence conflicts.
Done when: Demonstrate env beating file beating defaults in a screencast-style main() demo.
Q54 MEGA
Big Integer Arithmetic Engine
Your goal:

Train grade-school algorithm implementation and carrying/borrowing logic at scale.

What you must do:

Implement arbitrary-precision integer arithmetic using string or digit-list representation. Required operations: add, subtract (signed), multiply (grade-school + optional Karatsuba for bonus), compare, to_string(base 2-36). Benchmark against built-in int at 100, 500, 1000 digits. Handle negative numbers throughout with consistent sign rules.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No converting entire numbers to int for computation (only for verification in tests).
  • Karatsuba optional but earns bonus test suite.
  • Clear separation: DigitList utils, BigInt class, BaseConverter module.
  • Tests include edge cases: 0, -0, leading zeros, max carry chain.
Done when: Multiply two 200-digit numbers correctly; benchmark table printed.
Q55 MEGA
LEGB Scope Debugger Visualizer
Your goal:

Make scope resolution tangible — critical for closures, decorators, and class methods later.

What you must do:

Using the `inspect` module, build a tool that takes Python source (string or file) containing nested functions and prints a scope resolution trace for a given variable name at a given line. Show: local names, enclosing scopes chain, global names, whether `global`/`nonlocal` declared, and which binding would be used. Support at least 3 levels of nesting and class methods (bonus).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Parse with ast module; do not exec user code for analysis.
  • Output as ASCII tree or indented text.
  • Include 8 example programs in tests with expected resolution answers.
Done when: Correctly identifies UnboundLocalError cases before runtime.
Q56 MEGA
Propositional Logic Engine
Your goal:

Combine parsing, recursion, and truth tables — logic stamina for DSA proofs.

What you must do:

Build a system accepting formulas like `(A & B) | ~C`, parsing into AST, generating full truth tables, simplifying via basic identities (double negation, De Morgan, absorption), and checking tautology/contradiction. Support variables A-Z and operators: AND (&), OR (|), NOT (~), IMPLIES (>), IFF (<->).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Tokenizer + recursive descent parser; AST node classes.
  • Truth table for n variables (warn if n>8).
  • Simplifier applies rules until fixed point or max iterations.
  • Export truth table as CSV and Markdown.
Done when: Correctly classifies `(A > B) <-> (~B > ~A)` as tautology.
Q57 MEGA
Plugin Architecture With Entry-Point Pattern
Your goal:

Understand namespace, discovery, and registration — used in pytest, setuptools, Flask.

What you must do:

Create a mini plugin system: `@register_plugin('name')` decorator stores callables in a registry. Auto-discover plugins in a `plugins/` package using pkgutil.iter_modules. Build a CLI `app.py --list` and `app.py --run plugin_name args...`. Plugins implement Protocol with `name`, `run(argv)`, `description`. Include 3 sample plugins (hash, encode, stats).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Lazy loading — plugin code imported only when run.
  • Duplicate name registration raises clear error.
  • Plugin failure isolated — one bad plugin doesn't crash list command.
Done when: Third-party style plugin addable by dropping file in plugins/ folder.
Q58 MEGA
Bytecode Annotator & Learning Tool
Your goal:

Connect Python source to execution model — invaluable for performance and debugging.

What you must do:

Build `annotate_bytecode(func)` using `dis` module: for each opcode, print offset, opcode name, argument, and a plain-English sentence explaining what it does in context (e.g. 'LOAD_FAST 0 — push local variable x onto stack'). Support disassembly of nested functions and lambdas. Generate side-by-side source line mapping where possible (inspect.getsource + line numbers).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cover all opcodes in a sample function of at least 15 instructions.
  • Handle functions with closures (LOAD_DEREF explained).
  • CLI: `python bytecode_tool.py path/to/file.py function_name`.
Done when: A beginner can read output and trace execution of `for i in range(3): print(i)`.
Q59 MEGA
Roman Numeral Bidirectional Converter + Validator
Your goal:

Practice validation state machines and edge-case-heavy string logic.

What you must do:

Implement `to_roman(n: int) -> str` for 1..3999 and `from_roman(s: str) -> int` with strict validation. Reject: invalid chars, subtractive pairs wrong (IL, IC, etc.), more than 3 repeats, empty string. Build a fuzz tester generating random ints, round-trip to roman and back. Add CLI subcommands: convert-to, convert-from, validate, fuzz N.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Validation returns structured errors (code + message), not bare False.
  • Document subtractive notation rules in module docstring.
  • Fuzz catches zero failures on 10_000 random ints.
Done when: from_roman(to_roman(n)) == n for all n in 1..3999; invalid inputs caught.
Q60 MEGA
Deep Equality & Structural Diff Tool
Your goal:

Master graph traversal, cycles, and custom comparison — core of testing frameworks.

What you must do:

Implement `deep_equal(a, b, *, float_tol=1e-9) -> bool` WITHOUT using recursive `==` on containers. Handle: nested list/dict/set/tuple, mixed types, floats with tolerance, custom objects with __eq__, cyclic references (same object appearing twice). BONUS: `deep_diff(a, b) -> list[str]` returning human-readable path diffs like `root['users'][2]['age']: 30 != 31`.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cycle detection via id() visited sets per side.
  • Dict compare order-independent; set compare order-independent.
  • 20+ tests including mutual cycles and float edge cases.
  • deep_diff bonus: at least 10 diff path tests.
Done when: deep_equal handles `a=[1]; a.append(a); b=[1]; b.append(b)` correctly as True.

02 — Control Flow, Loops & Iteration

Topic Map: Conditionals → Iterator protocol → for/while → break/continue/else → match/case → Itertools patterns → DSA loop templates

1. Conditional Execution

1.1 if / elif / else

No switch before 3.10. Use elif chains or dict-based dispatch for many branches.

if score >= 90:
    grade = "A"
elif score >= 80:
    grade = "B"
else:
    grade = "C"

1.2 Conditional expression (ternary)

status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"

1.3 match / case — structural pattern matching (3.10+)

match data:
    case []:
        print("empty")
    case [x]:                    # single element
        print(f"one: {x}")
    case [first, *rest]:          # capture remainder
        print(first, rest)
    case {"type": "user", "id": uid}:
        fetch_user(uid)
    case Point(x=0, y=y) if y > 0:   # class pattern + guard
        print(f"positive y-axis: {y}")
    case _:
        print("default")

Unlike C switch: patterns are structural (shape of data), not just constant values.

2. The Iterator Protocol — Heart of for

# for x in obj:  desugars to:
_iterator = iter(obj)       # calls obj.__iter__()
while True:
    try:
        x = next(_iterator)  # calls _iterator.__next__()
    except StopIteration:
        break
TermDefinitionProtocol
IterableCan produce iterator__iter__() → iterator
IteratorStateful cursor__iter__ returns self, __next__ advances
SequenceOrdered, indexable__getitem__, __len__

3. while Loops

while condition:
    # body
else:
    # runs only if NO break occurred
    print("completed normally")

4. break, continue, pass, else

StatementBehavior
breakExit innermost loop; skips loop else
continueSkip to next iteration
passNo-op placeholder
else on loopRuns if loop finished without break
for n in range(2, 100):
    for d in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1):
        if n % d == 0:
            break
    else:
        print(n, "is prime")  # only if inner loop never broke

5. Iteration Utilities

range(5)              # 0,1,2,3,4 — lazy, O(1) memory
range(2, 10, 3)       # 2, 5, 8

for i, v in enumerate(items, start=1):
    print(i, v)

for a, b in zip(xs, ys, strict=True):  # 3.10+: raise if lengths differ
    ...

list(zip([1,2], [3]))  # [(1,3), (2,None)] without strict — silent truncate!

6. DSA Loop Templates (memorize these)

6.1 Two pointers

l, r = 0, len(arr) - 1
while l < r:
    if condition: l += 1
    else: r -= 1

6.2 Sliding window

start = 0
for end in range(len(s)):
    add(s[end])
    while not valid():
        remove(s[start]); start += 1
    update_answer()

6.3 Fast & slow (Floyd cycle)

slow = fast = head
while fast and fast.next:
    slow = slow.next
    fast = fast.next.next
Gotchas:
  • Never modify list size while iterating forward with index — use copy, reverse, or comprehension
  • for i in range(len(lst)) is usually worse than enumerate(lst)
  • Nested loop else attaches to innermost loop only
How to think about this topic: Every for loop is a conversation with an iterator. Your job is not "repeat code N times" — it's "pull next item until StopIteration." For DSA, map loop patterns to templates: two-pointer, sliding window, nested with invariant, state machine with explicit stack.

Common blockers (and fixes)

  • "for/else confuses me" → else = "loop finished without break." Draw break vs no-break paths.
  • "Nested loops melt my brain" → name the invariant: what must be true each outer iteration?
  • "match/case syntax" → read as "does data have this shape?" not "equals this value."

Connects forward

→ Comprehensions compress loops. → Recursion replaces some loops with call stack. → Generators make iteration lazy. → DSA: 70% of array problems use two-pointer or sliding window.

Key Takeaways:
  1. for = iterator protocol. Custom classes need __iter__/__next__.
  2. Two-pointer and sliding-window solve huge classes of array/string problems.
  3. match/case replaces ugly if/elif chains for structured data.
  4. Loop else + break = elegant "not found" pattern.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: fundamentals

Q1 HARD
Dispatch table mimicking match/case pre-3.10.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in control flow by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Dispatch table mimicking match/case pre-3.10. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Q50 BRUTAL
Bytecode VM 20+ opcodes.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in control flow by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Bytecode VM 20+ opcodes. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Stack-Based Bytecode Virtual Machine
Your goal:

Hold an entire execution model in your head — fetch-decode-execute loop for hours-long logic.

What you must do:

Implement a stack VM with ≥25 opcodes: PUSH, POP, DUP, ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, NEG, JMP, JMP_IF_FALSE, CALL, RET, LOAD_VAR, STORE_VAR, LOAD_CONST, CMP_EQ/LT/GT, PRINT, HALT. Write an assembler that converts simple text instructions to bytecode. Write programs in assembly: factorial, fibonacci, sum 1..n, collatz steps. Include disassembler printing human-readable trace during execution (debug mode).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • VM state: stack (list), ip (instruction pointer), globals dict, call stack for CALL/RET.
  • Assembler syntax documented with 5 example programs.
  • Debug mode prints stack after each instruction.
  • 25+ tests on individual opcodes and 3 full programs.
Done when: Assembly program computes fib(10) correctly; debug trace matches manual simulation.
Q52 MEGA
JSON Parser Using Explicit State Machine
Your goal:

Long-horizon parsing logic without recursive descent — mirrors real protocol parsers.

What you must do:

Parse JSON from str/bytes using while-loop + explicit stack (no json module, no eval). Support: objects, arrays, strings with escapes \n \t \" \\, unicode \uXXXX, numbers int/float, true/false/null, whitespace. Return Python objects; on error raise JsonParseError with line, column, and expected token.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Single-pass or two-pass allowed; document choice.
  • Handle nested depth ≥100 without recursion limit issues (use explicit stack).
  • 20+ tests including RFC 8259 edge cases from json.org examples.
  • Pretty-print error context: show line with caret at column.
Done when: Parses nested object 100 levels deep; rejects trailing commas with clear error.
Q53 MEGA
Pattern Match Desugarer (match → if/elif)
Your goal:

Understand structural matching by implementing the translation yourself.

What you must do:

Using ast module, parse Python 3.10+ source containing match/case statements. Transform match/case AST into equivalent if/elif/else chain as source code string. Support: literal patterns, capture patterns, wildcard, sequence patterns with *rest, guard clauses `if condition`, OR patterns `case A | B`. CLI reads file, prints desugared code, optionally exec both and compare outputs on test inputs.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Use ast.parse and ast.NodeTransformer or manual walk.
  • Round-trip tests: original and desugared produce same results on 10+ inputs.
  • Document unsupported patterns clearly.
Done when: Desugars simple HTTP status match example correctly; tests pass.
Q54 MEGA
Event-Driven Simulation Engine
Your goal:

Replace nested loops with priority queue driven simulation — long-running control flow.

What you must do:

Build discrete-event simulator: events have timestamp, type, payload. Priority queue (heapq) orders events. Loop: pop earliest, process handler, schedule follow-up events. Simulate: multi-server queue (customers arrive Poisson, service time configurable), track average wait time, queue length over time. Output timeline log and summary statistics.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No recursive event processing — explicit while loop until queue empty or max_time.
  • Configurable via dict: arrival_rate, service_time, num_servers, duration.
  • Reproducible with random.seed.
  • Export event log to CSV.
Done when: Increasing servers decreases avg wait time in demo scenario.
Q55 MEGA
Indentation-Based Language Parser (Mini-Python Subset)
Your goal:

Combine loops, stacks, and state for significant line-count logic.

What you must do:

Parse indented language subset: variable assignment, if/elif/else, while, print expr. Tokenizer + INDENT/DEDENT generation (stack comparing indent levels). Build AST, then interpreter with environment dict. Support expressions: numbers, strings, bool, arithmetic, comparisons, variable lookup.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • INDENT/DEDENT algorithm documented (compare to tokenize module behavior).
  • Syntax errors report line number.
  • 10 example programs including nested if and while loops.
  • 200+ lines total expected.
Done when: Runs program computing factorial via while loop with indentation.
Q56 MEGA
Collatz Research Pipeline
Your goal:

Optimize loops for performance while preserving correctness — DSA mindset.

What you must do:

For all n from 1 to 10^6, compute Collatz stopping time (steps to reach 1). Find n with maximum stopping time in range. Implement: naive loop, cached (memo) version, bit-optimized version (bonus). Visualize distribution histogram (text-based bars or export CSV). Parallelize range chunks with multiprocessing and compare wall time.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Verify all three methods agree on 10^4 sample.
  • Memo version must show measurable speedup.
  • Document longest chain found and its length.
Done when: Full 10^6 run completes in reasonable time with memo; histogram printed.
Q57 MEGA
Loop Invariant Verifier (Educational)
Your goal:

Practice reasoning about loop correctness — critical for binary search bugs.

What you must do:

Given Python source of a loop with annotated `# invariant: <expr>` comments before loop and before continue statements, verify (symbolically or by testing) invariant holds: (1) before first iteration if while-condition true, (2) after each iteration if continuing. Use ast to find loop, extract invariant expression, evaluate in isolated namespace with concrete random states (property-style testing).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Support while and for loops over range.
  • Report counterexample state when invariant fails.
  • 5 annotated example loops in test suite (binary search, sum array, etc.).
Done when: Catches incorrect binary search loop invariant when deliberately broken.
Q58 MEGA
Multi-Phase ETL Loop Pipeline
Your goal:

Chain many loop stages — mirrors data engineering control flow.

What you must do:

Process 1M+ fake log lines from generator (don't store all in memory): Phase 1 filter valid lines, Phase 2 parse fields, Phase 3 aggregate by hour and status code, Phase 4 compute error rate per hour, Phase 5 emit top 10 worst hours. Each phase is a generator function; wire with pipeline composition. Profile memory stays O(1) relative to 1M lines.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Use generator pipeline only — no list of 1M lines.
  • Include corrupt lines (test error handling skip/count).
  • Print aggregate stats at end.
Done when: Processes 1M lines without MemoryError; corrupt line count reported.
Q59 MEGA
Game Loop & Entity Component System Lite
Your goal:

Sustain long main-loop logic with multiple subsystems updating each tick.

What you must do:

Implement ECS-lite: World holds entities (ids), components (dict of type→data), systems (functions). Main loop 60 ticks: InputSystem, MovementSystem, CollisionSystem, RenderSystem (print ASCII grid). Entities move on grid; collision reverses direction; user input changes velocity from stdin non-blocking or scripted. Run 100 ticks, log entity positions.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Systems iterate entities without nested entity×system explosion (efficient component lookup).
  • Clean separation: no movement logic in render.
  • At least 5 entities, 10x10 grid.
Done when: Entities bounce off walls; 100 ticks complete with coherent log.
Q60 MEGA
Matrix Computation Loop Nest Optimizer Demo
Your goal:

Understand cache-friendly loop ordering through measurement.

What you must do:

Implement matrix multiply N×N for N up to 512 three ways: ijk, ikj, jik order. Benchmark each ordering, explain results using row/column major access patterns. Add blocked/tiled version (block size parameter) and show improvement. Plot or print table: N vs time for all variants.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Pure Python or numpy-free for core compare (numpy optional for verification).
  • Warmup runs before timing.
  • Written analysis paragraph explaining fastest variant.
Done when: Tiled version faster than naive ijk at N=256 on typical machine.

03 — Strings, bytes, bytearray

Topic Map: str internals → bytes/bytearray → encoding/decoding → formatting → methods → Unicode → performance → text algorithms

1. str — Immutable Unicode Sequence

Python 3 strings are Unicode code points. CPython 3.3+ uses PEP 393 flexible representation: 1, 2, or 4 bytes per character depending on content (latin-1 / UCS-2 / UCS-4).

1.1 Creation & literals

s = "hello"
s = 'hello'
s = """multi
line"""
s = r"raw\n"      # backslashes literal
s = f"value={x}"   # f-string (3.6+)
s = "café"         # Unicode directly in source (UTF-8 file encoding)

1.2 Immutability consequences

s = "abc"
s[0] = "A"         # TypeError — cannot assign
s += "def"         # creates NEW string object (old one may be discarded)

2. Performance — Concatenation Trap

# BAD: O(n²) total — each += copies entire string
result = ""
for s in huge_list:
    result += s

# GOOD: O(n) — single allocation
result = "".join(huge_list)

3. bytes vs bytearray vs str

TypeMutableElementsUse case
strNoUnicode code pointsText processing
bytesNoIntegers 0–255Raw binary, network, files
bytearrayYesIntegers 0–255Mutable binary buffer
"hello".encode("utf-8")     # b'hello'
b"hello".decode("utf-8")     # "hello"
b"\xff".decode("utf-8", errors="replace")  # replacement char on error
b"\xff".decode("utf-8", errors="ignore")   # skip bad bytes

4. Encoding & Decoding Deep Dive

  • UTF-8: variable 1–4 bytes, ASCII-compatible, default for Python 3 source and I/O.
  • ASCII: 0–127 only. Cannot encode é.
  • latin-1: maps byte value directly to Unicode U+0000..U+00FF.
len("é")           # 1 character
len("é".encode())  # 2 bytes in UTF-8
"💻".encode("utf-8")  # 4 bytes

5. String Formatting — Three Generations

# 1. %-formatting (legacy)
"%.2f" % 3.14159

# 2. str.format
"{name}: {value:.2f}".format(name="pi", value=3.14159)
"{:>10}".format("hi")   # right-align width 10

# 3. f-strings (preferred — fastest, most readable)
name, value = "pi", 3.14159
f"{name}: {value:.2f}"
f"{x=}"   # debug: 'x=42' (3.8+)

5.1 Format spec mini-language

f"{n:10.2f}"   # width 10, 2 decimal floats
f"{n:_}"       # thousands separator
f"{n:0>5}"     # zero-pad left to width 5
f"{pct:.1%}"   # percentage

6. Essential String Methods (know complexity)

MethodPurposeNotes
split(sep, maxsplit)Split to listsplit() splits on any whitespace
join(iterable)Concatenatesep.join(parts)
strip/lstrip/rstripRemove charsDefault: whitespace
replace(old, new, count)Replace substringImmutable — returns new str
find/indexSearchindex raises if not found
startswith/endswithPrefix/suffix testCan pass tuple of options
partition/rpartitionSplit onceReturns (before, sep, after)
casefold()Case-insensitive compareBetter than .lower() for Unicode

7. Unicode Essentials

import unicodedata
unicodedata.normalize("NFC", s)   # canonical composed
unicodedata.category("A")         # 'Lu' = uppercase letter
ord("A")   # 65
chr(65)    # "A"
Gotchas:
  • Comparing strings with == is case-sensitive; use casefold() for case-insensitive
  • split("") is invalid; use list(s) to split into chars
  • Reading text files: always specify encoding="utf-8"
  • Regex on Unicode: use re.UNICODE (default in py3)
How to think about this topic: Text is immutable sequences of Unicode code points. Binary (bytes) is a separate world — convert at the boundary (file read, network, encode/decode). For algorithms, strings are just arrays you can't mutate: two pointers, sliding window, and hashing all apply.

Common blockers

  • O(n²) concat → always ask: am I building a string in a loop? Use join.
  • Unicode confusion → one glyph ≠ one byte. Test with é, emoji, combining characters.
  • Regex first instinct → for interviews, manual two-pointer often clearer and faster to write.

Connects forward

→ Collections for char-frequency (Counter patterns). → Recursion for parse trees. → Q51+ MEGA: build lexer — same skills as DSA string parsing.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Strings immutable — use join for bulk concatenation.
  2. Text (str) vs binary (bytes) — encode/decode at I/O boundaries.
  3. f-strings are the modern formatting standard.
  4. String algorithms (KMP, two-pointer, sliding window) dominate DSA — master them in practice section.

Solve this challenge completely: Interview string compression. Before coding, spend 10 minutes writing pseudocode and listing edge cases. After coding, manually trace through 2 non-trivial examples line by line.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Edit-1 spell checker.

Q50 BRUTAL
Full lexer token stream+recovery.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in strings by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Full lexer token stream+recovery. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
  • Provide clear error messages with line/column on parse failure.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Unicode-Aware Text Normalization Pipeline
Your goal:

Master NFC/NFD, grapheme clusters, and collation edge cases that break naive string code.

What you must do:

Build a production-style text normalization library (250+ lines). PHASE 1 — Implement NFC, NFD, NFKC, NFKD using unicodedata without higher-level shortcuts for the core loop. PHASE 2 — Add a grapheme iterator that walks user-perceived characters (combine marks, emoji ZWJ sequences). PHASE 3 — Build compare_keys(a, b) for case-insensitive, accent-insensitive search using decomposition. PHASE 4 — CLI: normalize file, report byte length before/after, list codepoints for a substring. PHASE 5 — Document 5 real-world bugs (Turkish I, German ß, combining accents) with failing-then-passing tests.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No third-party unicode libraries; stdlib unicodedata only.
  • Grapheme tests include e+accent, flag emoji, family ZWJ sequence.
  • compare_keys must not use .lower() alone for Turkish locale demo.
  • Minimum 25 unit tests across phases.
  • CLI handles UTF-8 files with BOM.
Done when: NFC/NFD round-trip stable on test corpus; grapheme count matches external reference for 10 strings.
Q52 MEGA
Template Engine With Full Lexer-Parser-Render
Your goal:

Sustain multi-phase string parsing: tokenize, AST, evaluate — mirrors Jinja-style engines.

What you must do:

Implement mini template language in one package. Syntax: {{ expr }}, {% if cond %}...{% endif %}, {% for x in items %}...{% endfor %}, filters expr|upper, expr|default('x'). PHASE 1 — Tokenizer for text, variable, block tags. PHASE 2 — Parser builds AST nodes: Text, Output, If, For, FilterChain. PHASE 3 — Renderer walks AST with context dict; missing keys configurable strict/lenient. PHASE 4 — Compile templates to Python-like bytecode or nested functions for speed (optional cache). PHASE 5 — 10 fixture templates including nested for+if and filter chains.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No eval() or exec() on user templates.
  • Expression evaluator supports . attribute access and [index] on dict/list.
  • Error messages include template line number approximation.
  • Auto-escape HTML mode for {{ }} output when enabled.
  • 200+ lines; 20+ tests.
Done when: Renders nested order summary template with filters; strict mode raises on undefined variable.
Q53 MEGA
Diff Algorithm: Myers + Unified Patch Generator
Your goal:

Implement classic O(ND) string diff — long nested loops and careful index bookkeeping.

What you must do:

Implement Myers shortest edit script between two line lists (or char lists). PHASE 1 — Build edit graph exploration with furthest-reaching D-paths. PHASE 2 — Backtrack to produce op sequence: equal, insert, delete. PHASE 3 — Convert ops to unified diff format with @@ hunks @@ headers. PHASE 4 — Apply patch to base text; verify apply(diff(a,b), a) == b. PHASE 5 — CLI diff two files; optional --context N lines.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Pure Python; document time/space complexity.
  • Handle empty file, identical files, entirely different files.
  • Unified diff compatible with patch(1) structure (simplified OK if documented).
  • 15+ tests including symmetric and large line counts.
Done when: Generated patch reapplies cleanly; diff of file with itself is empty.
Q54 MEGA
Regex Engine Subset (NFA Simulation)
Your goal:

Understand backtracking and state sets — foundation for log parsing and validators.

What you must do:

Build regex matcher supporting: literals, ., *, +, ?, |, (), character classes [a-z], anchors ^ $. Use NFA with epsilon transitions OR Thompson construction. Simulate with active state set per input character. Add match, search (find first), findall with capture groups (numbered). Compare behavior on 20 patterns against re module (document intentional differences).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No import re for matching logic (re allowed only in tests).
  • Greedy * and + semantics documented.
  • Reject invalid patterns with ParseError and position.
  • Test catastrophic backtracking case and mitigations if any.
Done when: Matches (a|b)*abb on stress strings; capture group 1 returns correct substring.
Q55 MEGA
Log Parser & Structured Field Extractor
Your goal:

Chain many string operations on messy real-world text — ETL stamina.

What you must do:

Parse Apache/Nginx combined log format AND JSON-per-line logs from a generator producing 500k lines. PHASE 1 — Compiled regex or manual parser for combined log; extract IP, time, method, path, status, size. PHASE 2 — Validate IP (v4/v6), normalize path (collapse //, decode %20). PHASE 3 — Stream process: never load full file; aggregate status histogram, top paths, bytes served. PHASE 4 — Detect anomalies: 5xx spike, unusual user-agent patterns via simple string heuristics. PHASE 5 — Export summary JSON and top-20 tables as Markdown.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Generator-based streaming; O(1) memory vs line count.
  • Malformed lines counted and sampled in error report (max 10 examples).
  • Path normalization has 10+ unit tests.
  • Process 500k lines under 30s on typical laptop.
Done when: Aggregates match golden file on sample; malformed line count reported.
Q56 MEGA
Markdown Subset Compiler to HTML
Your goal:

Multi-pass string transforms: block structure then inline emphasis.

What you must do:

Support: ATX headers # ## ###, paragraphs, **bold**, *italic*, `code`, [link](url), unordered - lists, ordered 1. lists, fenced ``` code blocks, blockquote >. PHASE 1 — Block parser splits into Block nodes. PHASE 2 — Inline parser per block for emphasis/links/code. PHASE 3 — HTML renderer with escaping for raw text. PHASE 4 — Table of contents generator from headers. PHASE 5 — 15 markdown fixtures with expected HTML snapshots.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Escape <, >, & in non-code text.
  • Nested emphasis rules documented (e.g. *a **b** c*).
  • No third-party markdown libs.
  • 200+ lines minimum.
Done when: All fixtures match expected HTML; XSS test: raw <script> escaped in paragraph.
Q57 MEGA
Phonetic Encoder Suite (Soundex + Metaphone Lite)
Your goal:

Practice algorithmic string transforms with rule tables and state machines.

What you must do:

Implement Soundex and a simplified Metaphone for ASCII names. Soundex: preserve first letter, map consonants, collapse repeats, pad to 4. Metaphone lite: at least 10 substitution rules (PH->F, GH-> silent, etc.). Build deduplication tool: cluster names by phonetic key, pick canonical.display. Benchmark 100k names; compare collision rates between algorithms.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Handle empty string, punctuation, hyphenated names.
  • Cluster report: size, example names per cluster.
  • Document known limitations vs full Metaphone.
  • 12+ tests with reference outputs.
Done when: Robert/Rupert cluster together in demo; Soundex matches known reference cases.
Q58 MEGA
String Interning & Flyweight Text Table
Your goal:

Optimize memory for repeated strings in parsers and compilers.

What you must do:

Implement StringTable: intern(s) -> int id, get(id) -> str, stats() -> unique count, bytes saved estimate. Build parser for tiny DSL where identifiers and string literals are interned. Serialize AST with ids only; deserialize reconstructs via table. Compare memory: without intern vs with intern on document with 10k repeated tokens. Thread-safety optional bonus with locking.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • intern is idempotent: intern(s) == intern(s) same id.
  • Weak-value dict bonus: allow GC when str unused.
  • Serialization format documented (JSON or binary).
  • Measure RSS or sys.getsizeof proxy on benchmark.
Done when: Memory estimate shows savings on repetitive corpus; round-trip serialization lossless.
Q59 MEGA
CSV/TSV Robust Reader-Writer (RFC 4180)
Your goal:

Handle quoting, newlines in fields, and encoding — common production pain.

What you must do:

Implement csv reader/writer without csv module for core logic (csv ok in verification tests). Support: quoted fields, doubled quotes, delimiters in quotes, \r\n and \n row endings, custom delimiter and quote char, UTF-8 with optional BOM. Streaming reader yields row dicts when header provided. Writer escapes fields correctly. Fuzz: random cells with commas, quotes, newlines — round-trip.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • RFC 4180 test vectors included.
  • Dialect object: delimiter, quotechar, lineterminator.
  • Error on uneven column count with row number.
  • 18+ tests.
Done when: Fuzz 1000 random rows round-trip; RFC official examples pass.
Q60 MEGA
Palindrome & Pattern Research Engine on Large Corpus
Your goal:

Combine string algorithms with file I/O and performance tuning.

What you must do:

Scan word list (provided generator: 100k+ words). Find: longest palindrome word, all palindrome phrases (split words), anagram families (sort letters as key), isograms, heterograms. PHASE 1 — Efficient palindrome check; Manacher for longest palindromic substring in a line. PHASE 2 — Anagram hash map with bucketing. PHASE 3 — Multi-process chunk search for anagram families size >= 4. PHASE 4 — HTML report with tables and complexity notes.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Manacher O(n) for substring problem on each line.
  • Anagram key uses sorted tuple or letter count vector.
  • Multiprocessing speedup demonstrated on timer.
  • Report lists top 10 anagram families by size.
Done when: Manacher matches brute on random strings; largest anagram family found and documented.

04 — Lists, Tuples, Sets, Dicts

Topic Map: list → tuple → dict → set → complexity → slicing → copying → collections module → DSA structures

1. list — Dynamic Array

CPython list is a dynamic array of pointers to PyObject. Over-allocates for amortized O(1) append.

1.1 Complexity cheat sheet

OperationAverageNotes
append(x)O(1)Amortized
pop()O(1)End only
pop(0)O(n)Shifts all elements
insert(i, x)O(n)
x in lstO(n)Linear search
lst[i]O(1)Index access
lst[a:b]O(b-a)Shallow copy of slice
sort()O(n log n)Timsort — stable

1.2 Slicing — views vs copies

lst = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
lst[1:4]      # [1, 2, 3]
lst[::-1]     # reverse
lst[::2]      # every 2nd element
lst[:]        # shallow copy (new list, same inner objects)

2. tuple — Immutable Sequence

point = (3, 4)
x, y = point           # unpacking
a, *rest, z = (1,2,3,4) # a=1, rest=[2,3], z=4

# hashable if ALL elements hashable → can be dict key
{(1, 2): "value"}      # OK
{([1, 2]): "bad"}       # TypeError — list inside

3. dict — Hash Table

Since Python 3.7, dicts preserve insertion order (official language guarantee since 3.7).

3.1 Requirements for keys

Keys must be hashable: immutable types with stable __hash__ for lifetime. If a == b then hash(a) == hash(b).

3.2 Dict operations

d = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
d["c"] = 3                    # insert/update O(1) avg
d.get("x", default)           # safe lookup
d.setdefault("a", 0)          # insert if missing
d.pop("a")                    # remove and return
d1 | d2                       # merge (3.9+), d1 unchanged
{**d1, **d2}                  # merge via unpacking

for k in d: ...               # iterate keys
for k, v in d.items(): ...    # key-value pairs

3.3 Dict comprehension

{word: len(word) for word in words}
{k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > 0}

4. set & frozenset

s = {1, 2, 3}
s.add(4)
s.remove(1)       # KeyError if missing
s.discard(99)     # no error if missing

a | b   # union
a & b   # intersection
a - b   # difference
a ^ b   # symmetric difference

# O(1) average membership — use for dedup and fast lookup
seen = set()
if x not in seen:
    seen.add(x)

5. Copying Nested Structures

import copy
a = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
b = a[:]           # shallow — inner lists shared
c = copy.deepcopy(a)  # fully independent

6. collections Module Essentials

TypePurpose
dequeO(1) append/pop both ends — queues, BFS
CounterCount hashable elements — most_common(n)
defaultdictAuto-default on missing key
namedtupleTuple with named fields
OrderedDictMostly obsolete — plain dict is ordered now

7. array.array — Homogeneous C Arrays

import array
nums = array.array('i', [1, 2, 3])  # 'i' = signed int
# Much less memory than list of ints for large numeric data
Gotchas:
  • [[0]*n]*m creates m references to SAME inner list — use [[0]*n for _ in range(m)]
  • Mutating dict/set while iterating — iterate over list(d) copy
  • set requires hashable elements — no lists inside
  • Dict keys must not change hash value during lifetime
How to think about this topic: Pick structure by operation you need most: O(1) lookup → dict/set. Order + index → list. Fixed record → tuple. Queue BFS → deque. This is the toolkit for 80% of DSA problems — before coding, ask "what operation must be fast?" and choose accordingly.

Common blockers

  • [[0]*n]*m trap → draw reference diagram; inner lists are shared.
  • dict key errors → use .get() or defaultdict until you prove key exists.
  • When list vs deque → pop(0) on list is O(n); deque popleft is O(1).

Connects forward

→ Memory: slicing copies references. → Functions: pass by reference semantics. → Recursion on trees built from dicts/lists. → DSA: hash map + heap = top patterns.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Know Big-O for every list/dict/set operation — interview essential.
  2. Dict + set power 80% of DSA lookup problems.
  3. deque for BFS; heapq for priority queues; defaultdict for graphs.
  4. Shallow vs deep copy matters with nested mutable data.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: fundamentals, control-flow, strings

Q1 HARD
Implement dynamic array (list) with resize doubling.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in collections by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement dynamic array (list) with resize doubling. Start by listing inputs/outputs on paper, then edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Your solution must be runnable end-to-end, not a snippet.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — do not use high-level stdlib shortcuts unless the question explicitly allows it.
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.

Q16 HARD
namedtuple without collections.
Your goal:

Q50 BRUTAL
In
Your goal:

Master a core skill in collections by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: In-memory database (tables, indexes, query subset). Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
Ordered Dict LRU Cache From Scratch
Your goal:

Implement doubly-linked list + hash map — interview capstone that becomes real cache code.

What you must do:

Build LRUCache(maxsize) with O(1) get/set using your own Node list and dict (not OrderedDict.move_to_end). PHASE 1 — DoublyLinkedList with insert, remove, move_to_front. PHASE 2 — LRUCache eviction on capacity, update on get. PHASE 3 — TTL variant: entries expire after seconds (lazy expiry on access + periodic sweep). PHASE 4 — Thread-safe wrapper with RLock. PHASE 5 — @lru_cache-like decorator built on your cache with statistics hits/misses/evictions.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • get and set average O(1); document invariants.
  • TTL tests use time mocking or controllable clock injection.
  • Thread test: 4 threads, 10k ops, no lost updates.
  • 25+ unit tests.
Done when: Cache behavior matches functools.lru_cache on test functions; stats accurate.
Q52 MEGA
Trie + Autocomplete + Spell Check System
Your goal:

Tree traversal at scale with prefix queries — search UX backbone.

What you must do:

Implement Trie with insert, search, starts_with, delete. Add frequency weights per word for ranking autocomplete top-k. Spell checker: within edit distance 2 (Levenshtein) using trie pruning. Load dictionary from file; interactive CLI loop for prefix suggestions. Serialize trie to disk and reload without rebuilding from scratch.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Autocomplete returns k results sorted by frequency then alpha.
  • Delete compresses nodes when possible.
  • Levenshtein uses DP with trie-guided branch cut.
  • 200+ lines; 20+ tests.
Done when: Prefix 'py' suggests python/pypi/pytest per frequencies; spell fixes 'speling' -> 'spelling'.
Q53 MEGA
Custom defaultdict / counter / deque Teaching Library
Your goal:

Rebuild collections abstractions to understand their internals.

What you must do:

Reimplement: MyDefaultDict (default_factory), MyCounter (most_common, elements, arithmetic), MyDeque (maxlen optional, O(1) append/pop both ends on list-backed with blocks OR ring buffer). Each class passes subset of CPython test scenarios you write. Write essay in module docstring comparing your deque vs list for queue workloads.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • MyCounter supports +, -, &, | with documented semantics.
  • MyDeque maxlen evicts from opposite end.
  • No subclassing collections.deque for core logic.
  • 18+ tests per class minimum combined.
Done when: MyCounter.most_common(3) matches collections.Counter on 10 fixtures.
Q54 MEGA
Graph ADT With Multiple Adjacency Representations
Your goal:

Choose structures wisely — dict of sets vs matrix affects every algorithm.

What you must do:

Build Graph class supporting directed/undirected, weighted edges. Representations: adjacency list (default), edge list, adjacency matrix (lazy build). Algorithms: BFS, DFS (iterative+recursive), Dijkstra, topological sort, connected components. PHASE 1 — API: add_vertex, add_edge, neighbors, edges. PHASE 2 — All algorithms return structured results (paths, distances). PHASE 3 — Import/export GraphML-like JSON. PHASE 4 — Visualize small graph as ASCII adjacency list dump.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Dijkstra handles unreachable nodes as inf distance.
  • Topo sort detects cycle and raises.
  • Compare memory of representations on 1000-node sparse vs dense doc.
  • 22+ tests.
Done when: Shortest path on weighted demo matches manual calculation; cycle detected in topo.
Q55 MEGA
Priority Queue Scheduler With Stable Ordering
Your goal:

Heap mastery for event systems and job queues.

What you must do:

Implement MinHeap and MaxHeap on list with sift up/down. StablePriorityQueue: ties broken by insertion order (use counter tie-breaker). Build job scheduler: jobs have priority, deadline, runnable function name (simulate). Process until queue empty: pop highest priority, run, log wait time. Support decrease-key (bonus) for deadline updates.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Heapify/build_heap O(n) included.
  • No heapq module in heap implementation (heapq OK in tests).
  • Stable order verified when all priorities equal.
  • Scheduler logs: job id, start, end, latency.
Done when: 100 equal-priority jobs finish FIFO; scheduler meets deadline order on test set.
Q56 MEGA
Union-Find (Disjoint Set) With Path Compression
Your goal:

Near-constant amortized ops — used in Kruskal, image processing, networks.

What you must do:

Implement UnionFind with union by rank and path compression. Applications: detect cycle in undirected graph during edge insertion, count connected components dynamically, Kruskal MST on 500-edge graph, 2D grid 'number of islands' with union per land cell. Benchmark union/find sequence 1e6 ops vs naive list parent.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • find returns canonical root; compress path.
  • MST returns total weight and edge list.
  • Islands test matches DFS count on 20 grids.
  • Timing table printed for naive vs optimized.
Done when: 1e6 ops complete faster than naive by wide margin; MST weight correct on fixture.
Q57 MEGA
Bloom Filter + Count-Min Sketch Library
Your goal:

Probabilistic collections for big data streams.

What you must do:

BloomFilter: configurable m bits, k hash functions (use hashlib seeds). Support add, probably_contains, false positive rate estimate. CountMinSketch: increment, estimate frequency, top-k heavy hitters from stream. Simulate 1M URL stream with 10k unique; compare CMS estimates to exact Counter. Write report on FP rate vs theoretical.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cannot use pybloom or third-party probabilistic libs.
  • Plot or print FP rate vs number of inserted elements.
  • CMS width/depth configurable; document error bound.
  • 15+ tests on synthetic streams.
Done when: Bloom FP rate near theory at 50% capacity; CMS within 2x of true for heavy hitters.
Q58 MEGA
Immutable Persistent Vector (HAMT Lite)
Your goal:

Structural sharing — functional collections used in Clojure-like models.

What you must do:

Implement append-only PVector with set(index, val) returning NEW vector sharing structure. Use tree of arrays (fanout 16 or 32); path copying on update. Implement iterator, len, index access O(log n). Compare copy cost: list copy vs persistent set on 10k updates. Bonus: PMap with similar persistence.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Old versions unchanged after new update (structural sharing test).
  • No deepcopy for updates.
  • Depth bounded; document fanout choice.
  • 12+ tests including branching stress.
Done when: 10 versions coexist with shared nodes; index get/set correct on 50k elements.
Q59 MEGA
Multi-Index In-Memory Table
Your goal:

Mirror database indexes using dicts and sets — query planning logic.

What you must do:

Table stores records as dicts with schema. Create indexes: hash on field, sorted list on numeric field, composite key tuple index. Query mini-language: eq, lt, gt, and, or on indexed fields — use indexes when possible. Explain query plan: FULL_SCAN vs INDEX. Insert 100k records; compare query times indexed vs not.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • At least 3 index types implemented.
  • Query planner picks best index (simple heuristic OK, document).
  • Updates maintain index consistency.
  • Benchmark printed for 3 queries.
Done when: Indexed query 10x+ faster on 100k table for selective predicate; plan logged.
Q60 MEGA
Time-Series Ring Buffer Aggregator
Your goal:

Fixed-memory collections for metrics — deque + aggregation chains.

What you must do:

RingBufferBucket: fixed window of (timestamp, value) pairs per metric name. Roll up: min, max, avg, p95 (approximate with histogram or sorted window). Support multiple metrics in MetricsRegistry. Ingest 1M samples across 100 metrics; never exceed configured memory. Export Prometheus-style text exposition format.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • O(1) amortized insert per sample into ring.
  • p95 within 5% of exact on stationary test data.
  • Memory cap: evict oldest globally or per-metric policy documented.
  • Export format valid per documented subset.
Done when: 1M inserts bounded memory; exposition parses by simple regex test.

05 — Functions, Lambda & Closures

Topic Map: Def syntax → Parameters → *args/**kwargs → First-class functions → Closures → Lambda → Higher-order patterns

1. Function Definition Anatomy

def function_name(pos_only, /, standard, *args, kw_only, **kwargs):
    """Docstring - available as func.__doc__"""
    return result

1.1 Parameter kinds (3.8+)

SyntaxKindExample call
aPositional or keywordf(1) or f(a=1)
a, /Positional-onlyf(1) only
*, bKeyword-only after *f(b=2) only
*argsExtra positional tuple
**kwargsExtra keyword dict

1.2 Default arguments — THE trap

# WRONG — list created ONCE at definition time
def append_item(x, lst=[]):
    lst.append(x)
    return lst

append_item(1)  # [1]
append_item(2)  # [1, 2]  ← surprise!

# CORRECT
def append_item(x, lst=None):
    if lst is None:
        lst = []
    lst.append(x)
    return lst

2. Functions Are First-Class Objects

Functions can be assigned, passed as arguments, returned, stored in data structures.

def greet(name): return f"Hello, {name}"

say_hi = greet          # alias
funcs = [greet, len, print]
funcs[0]("World")

def apply(fn, x): return fn(x)
apply(len, [1,2,3])     # 3

3. Closures

Inner function captures variables from enclosing scope by reference (late binding).

def make_multiplier(n):
    def multiply(x):
        return x * n   # n captured from enclosing scope
    return multiply

double = make_multiplier(2)
double(5)  # 10

3.1 Late-binding loop trap

# BUG — all lambdas return 2 (i bound by reference, final value)
funcs = [lambda: i for i in range(3)]

# FIX — capture current value via default arg
funcs = [lambda i=i: i for i in range(3)]

4. Lambda — Anonymous Single-Expression Functions

square = lambda x: x ** 2
sorted(items, key=lambda x: x[1])
# Limitation: ONE expression only — no statements, no assignments (pre-3.8)

5. Unpacking in Calls

def f(a, b, c): ...

args = [1, 2, 3]
f(*args)           # unpack list/tuple as positional

kwargs = {"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}
f(**kwargs)        # unpack dict as keyword

f(1, *[2, 3])      # mixed
f(*[1], **{"b": 2, "c": 3})

6. Return Values

def stats(nums):
    return min(nums), max(nums), sum(nums)/len(nums)  # tuple

lo, hi, avg = stats([1,2,3])  # unpacking

7. Function Attributes & Introspection

def f(x: int) -> str: ...
f.__name__       # 'f'
f.__annotations__  # {'x': int, 'return': str}
f.__defaults__   # default arg values tuple

import inspect
inspect.signature(f)  # full signature object
Gotchas:
  • Mutable defaults — see above
  • Closure in loop without default-arg capture
  • return without value returns None
  • Functions without return implicitly return None
How to think about this topic: Functions are objects that create a new scope frame when called. Parameters are local names bound to passed object references — not copies. Closures capture variables from outer frames by reference (late binding in loops!). Decorators (next major topic) are just functions that wrap functions.

Common blockers

  • Mutable default → default evaluated once at def time. Draw timeline: def → call → call.
  • Lambda in loop → all close over same variable; fix with default arg capture.
  • *args vs **kwargs → * collects extra positional tuple; ** collects extra keyword dict.

Connects forward

→ Decorators = functions(factory(function)). → Generators = functions with yield. → OOP methods are functions with self bound.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Functions are objects — pass them around freely.
  2. Closures capture by reference; understand late binding in loops.
  3. Never use mutable defaults; use None sentinel pattern.
  4. Master *args, **kwargs, and unpacking for flexible APIs.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: collections

Q1 HARD
Fix closure late-binding loop lambda bug 5 ways.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in functions by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

Debug and fix: Fix closure late-binding loop lambda bug 5 ways. Show the broken code, explain precisely why it fails (reference LEGB, binding rules, etc.), then provide 3 distinct correct fixes with trade-offs.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
