  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: Runs correctly on normal + edge inputs; tests included; another developer could use your code without asking questions.
Q50 BRUTAL
Static analyzer 5 rules + report HTML.
Your goal:

Master a core skill in stdlib by applying concepts from the notes section — not memorizing syntax.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Static analyzer 5 rules + report HTML. Break work into phases: (1) design on paper/data structures, (2) core implementation, (3) error handling, (4) tests, (5) demo. Commit each phase. This is a multi-hour build meant to stretch your ability to hold long logic in your head.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone Python module under your topic practice folder.
  • Add at least 8–12 test cases (pytest or manual asserts) proving edge cases.
  • Include a `if __name__ == '__main__':` demo block showing real output.
  • Write docstrings explaining your approach and complexity where relevant.
Done when: All phases complete, ≥10 tests pass, demo runs without errors, code is split into logical functions/classes.
Q51 MEGA
argparse Multi-Subcommand CLI Application
Your goal:

Production CLI structure using stdlib argparse only.

What you must do:

Build `tool` CLI: subcommands init, run, status, config. Global flags: --verbose, --config path. run accepts positional script and --dry-run. config get/set/list nested keys. Shell completion hint file generator optional. Help text polished; exit codes documented.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • argparse subparsers with required subcommand.
  • Defaults from config file merged with CLI.
  • Invalid usage prints help snippet exit 2.
  • 20+ invoke tests subprocess or argparse API.
Done when: tool run --help works; wrong args exit 2 with message.
Q52 MEGA
logging Hierarchical Configuration System
Your goal:

Filters, handlers, formatters, dictConfig mastery.

What you must do:

Configure via logging.config.dictConfig YAML or JSON file. Handlers: console INFO, rotating file DEBUG, error file ERROR only. Filter suppresses noisy third_party logger. Context filter injects request_id from contextvars. Library module uses getLogger(__name__); demo propagation.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • dictConfig load from file.
  • RotatingFileHandler maxBytes backupCount.
  • Third party logger capped WARNING.
  • 15+ log capture tests.
Done when: ERROR appears in error log not console; rotation triggers on size.
Q53 MEGA
pathlib Project Scaffold Generator
Your goal:

Path operations cross-platform for tooling.

What you must do:

Generate project tree from template dict: dirs and files with content. Support variables {name}, {date}; .gitignore respect skip. Dry-run mode lists actions; apply mode creates atomically rollback on failure. Find all *.py files > 100 lines report. Sync two dirs: copy missing, optional delete extras.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • pathlib.Path throughout not os.path.
  • Rollback on mid-create exception.
  • Dry-run no filesystem changes.
  • 18+ tempdir tests.
Done when: Scaffold creates expected tree; rollback leaves no partial on error.
Q54 MEGA
datetime Timezone-Aware Scheduling Library
Your goal:

zoneinfo, timedelta, recurrence rules without dateutil.

What you must do:

Parse ISO8601 datetimes with offsets. Schedule events: daily, weekly, monthly nth weekday using datetime only. Convert series to UTC for storage display local. DST edge case tests: spring forward fall back America/New_York. Export iCal subset VEVENT strings.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • zoneinfo.ZoneInfo for IANA zones.
  • Naive datetime rejected at API boundary.
  • DST gap and fold documented behavior.
  • 20+ recurrence tests.
Done when: Weekly meeting skips DST gap correctly; iCal snippet valid structure.
Q55 MEGA
sqlite3 Data Access Layer With Migrations
Your goal:

stdlib sqlite3 for persistent app without ORM.

What you must do:

Connection factory row_factory sqlite3.Row. Migration runner SQL files numbered 001_init.sql. Repository CRUD users and posts FK. Parameterized queries only; SQL injection test fails safely. Backup API copies DB file with vacuum into.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Context manager connection commit/rollback.
  • Migration tracking table.
  • FK constraints enabled PRAGMA foreign_keys.
  • 25+ CRUD tests memory db.
Done when: Migrations apply once; injection attempt returns no extra rows.
Q56 MEGA
subprocess Pipeline Runner With Timeout & Capture
Your goal:

Shell out safely — no shell=True in production paths.

What you must do:

run_pipeline([['grep', pat], ['wc', '-l']], input=text) chaining stdout to stdin. Timeout per stage; kill process group on timeout. Capture stdout/stderr separate; return exit codes list. Dry-run prints argv only. Cross-platform: find git or skip on Windows documented.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • subprocess.run Popen with pipes.
  • No shell=True.
  • Timeout kills entire pipeline.
  • 12+ tests echo/cat equivalents.
Done when: Pipeline wc -l counts lines; timeout kills hung mock process.
Q57 MEGA
email MIME Multipart Builder & Parser
Your goal:

Compose and parse emails with attachments stdlib.

What you must do:

Build multipart/alternative plain+html email with attachment application/pdf bytes. Parse incoming bytes extract subject, bodies, save attachments to paths. Validate address parse formataddr. Handle charset UTF-8 headers encoded. Round-trip build parse compare.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • email.message EmailMessage API.
  • Attachment filename preserved.
  • Header encoding non-ASCII display name.
  • 15+ round-trip tests.
Done when: Parsed attachment bytes match original; subject decoded correctly.
Q58 MEGA
secrets & hashlib Security Utilities Module
Your goal:

Token generation, password hashing, constant-time compare.

What you must do:

generate_token(nbytes), hash_password(plain) with salt pbkdf2_hmac, verify_password. API key format prefix sk_live_ + urlsafe. HMAC sign request canonical string verify timing-safe. Document never use random module for secrets.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • secrets module for tokens.
  • hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac iterations >= 100_000.
  • hmac.compare_digest for verify.
  • 12+ security tests.
Done when: Wrong password fails verify; HMAC tamper detected.
Q59 MEGA
functools + itertools Pipeline Benchmark App
Your goal:

Compose stdlib functional tools on real dataset.

What you must do:

Load CSV with csv module; pipeline: filter rows, map transform, groupby key, reduce aggregate using functools.reduce and itertools.groupby. Partial application for configurable filters. lru_cache on expensive parse function. Report top 10 groups by sum field.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • csv.DictReader input.
  • groupby requires sort preprocessing documented.
  • lru_cache cache_info printed.
  • Process 100k rows under 5s.
Done when: Top 10 matches manual pandas-free reference on sample.
Q60 MEGA
unittest + mock Integration Test Suite
Your goal:

Test doubles, patches, fixtures for service layer.

What you must do:

Service calls external HTTP and filesystem. Tests use unittest.mock patch urlopen and open. setUp/tearDown temp dirs; TestCase submodules by feature. Doctest snippets in docstrings verified. Coverage-style report which methods tested (manual checklist).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • At least 30 test methods.
  • mock patch as context and decorator both used.
  • One TestCase skipped conditionally platform.
  • Doctest passes module examples.
Done when: All tests pass offline; mocks assert called_with correct args.

DSA & Developer Roadmap — After Python Mastery

Phase 1: DSA in Python (8–12 weeks)

  • Week 1–2: Arrays, strings, two pointers, sliding window — LeetCode medium daily.
  • Week 3–4: Linked lists, stacks, queues, hash maps — implement from scratch once.
  • Week 5–6: Trees, BST, heaps, tries — recursive + iterative.
  • Week 7–8: Graphs BFS/DFS/Dijkstra/Union-Find.
  • Week 9–10: DP classic 50 (knapsack, LIS, edit distance, grid paths).
  • Week 11–12: Greedy, intervals, bit manipulation, math.

Phase 2: Dev Skills

  • Git advanced (rebase, cherry-pick, bisect)
  • SQL + indexes + EXPLAIN
  • HTTP/REST + one framework (FastAPI recommended)
  • Testing (pytest, coverage, TDD on one project)
  • Docker + CI (GitHub Actions)
  • One portfolio project: 500+ lines, typed, tested, packaged

Daily Practice Protocol

  1. Morning (45 min): 2 new concept sections from this guide.
  2. Afternoon (90 min): 5 practice questions — write full solutions in practice/ folder.
  3. Evening (60 min): 1 LeetCode hard OR extend your capstone project.
  4. Weekend: Review all questions marked stuck; rewrite without looking.

Folder Structure for Your Practice

python/
  python-master.html          # this guide
  practice/
    01_fundamentals/
    02_control_flow/
    ...
    capstones/
      mini_interpreter/
      async_web_server/
  notes/                      # your own additions
  pyproject.toml

C++ MASTER

Memory-first C++ — 18 topics, 60 questions each. Order: compilation → pointers → RAII → OOP → STL → move → templates → bridge.

18
Topics
1080
Questions
RAII
Core feel
Rules: Build with -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic. Run ASan/UBSan. Solutions under practice_cpp/.

01 — Compilation Model, Headers & Toolchain

Topic Map: Preprocessor → Compile → Assemble → Link → Headers → ODR → Namespaces → Linkage

1. The four compilation stages

C++ source becomes an executable through distinct phases. Each .cpp file is a translation unit (TU) compiled independently, then linked together.

1.1 Preprocess

#include pastes headers, macros expand, #if branches resolve. Run g++ -E main.cpp to inspect.

1.2 Compile

Each TU becomes assembly; the compiler type-checks and optimizes per file only.

1.3 Assemble

Assembly → object file (.o / .obj) containing machine code + symbol table.

1.4 Link

Linker merges object files and libraries, resolves symbols, produces the executable.

g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra -O2 -g math.cpp main.cpp -o app
./app; echo $?   # exit code 0 = success

2. Headers and include guards

Headers declare; .cpp files define. Without guards, double inclusion duplicates declarations and breaks compilation.

// widget.h
#pragma once
class Widget {
    int val_{0};
public:
    explicit Widget(int v);
    int value() const;
};

// widget.cpp
#include "widget.h"
Widget::Widget(int v) : val_(v) {}
int Widget::value() const { return val_; }
Rule: Never #include a .cpp — you get duplicate symbols at link time unless every definition is inline.

3. One Definition Rule (ODR)

Every non-inline function and non-inline variable must have exactly one definition in the entire program.

// utils.h — OK: inline function, header-safe
inline int twice(int x) { return x * 2; }

// utils.h — declaration only; define once in utils.cpp
int add(int a, int b);

4. Linkage and namespaces

static at file scope = internal linkage. Anonymous namespace is the modern equivalent — symbols hidden from other TUs.

namespace app {
namespace detail { void helper(); }
namespace fs = std::filesystem;
}

5. Build hygiene

g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic
g++ -std=c++20 -g -O0 -fsanitize=address,undefined
Feel it: "undefined reference" = missing definition. "multiple definition" = ODR violation across TUs.
How to feel C++ from day one: C++ is compile contracts between files. Link errors = ODR/symbols. Runtime crashes = lifetime, not syntax typos.

Before Q1–Q60

  • Draw .h declares / .cpp defines / linker merges.
  • Explain #pragma once vs include guards.

Connects forward

→ Types/const adds type rules. → Pointers exposes addresses. → Everything is memory + ownership.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Each .cpp is one translation unit; headers are pasted, not linked separately.
  2. ODR: one definition per non-inline entity across the whole program.
  3. #pragma once or include guards prevent duplicate declarations from repeated #include.
  4. Internal linkage (static / anonymous namespace) hides implementation details from other TUs.
  5. Compile with -Wall -Wextra early — linker errors often trace back to missing inline or duplicate definitions.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: none

Q1 HARD
Trace the 4 compilation stages: preprocess, compile, assemble, link.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in fundamentals — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Analyze and predict exact behavior for: Trace the 4 compilation stages: preprocess, compile, assemble, link For each case write predicted output/UB/leak, then verify with compiler + sanitizer. Explain the underlying C++ rule (lifetime, ODR, overload, etc.) in 2–4 sentences each.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Written predictions match compiler/sanitizer output; no hand-waving.
Q2 HARD
Demonstrate ODR violation with duplicate non-inline function definitions across ...
Your goal:

Master core C++ in fundamentals — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Demonstrate ODR violation with duplicate non-inline function definitions across TUs; fix with inline/header-only. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.
Q3 BRUTAL
Build a 3-file project (main, utils.cpp, utils.h) using only g++ commands
Your goal:

Master core C++ in fundamentals — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Build a 3-file project (main, utils.cpp, utils.h) using only g++ commands — no CMake. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • CMake project must configure and build out-of-source (`cmake -B build && cmake --build build`).
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Q18 BRUTAL
Detect circular #include with incomplete types
Your goal:

Master core C++ in fundamentals — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Q43 BRUTAL
Mini build script (compile+link+test) without CMake
Your goal:

Master core C++ in fundamentals — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Mini build script (compile+link+test) without CMake — bash or Python driver. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • CMake project must configure and build out-of-source (`cmake -B build && cmake --build build`).
  • Provide Python test script importing the extension and asserting correctness.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.

  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.
Q49 BRUTAL
Benchmark unity build vs separate TUs on 10
Your goal:

Master core C++ in fundamentals — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Benchmark unity build vs separate TUs on 10-file synthetic project. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Print timing table for ≥3 input sizes; explain cache/allocator effects.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.

Q51 MEGA
Multi-TU Build Pipeline Without CMake
Your goal:

Internalize preprocess-compile-link as a pipeline you control.

What you must do:

Build a 5-file library with a driver running preprocess, compile, and link separately; emit preprocessed output and symbol report.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Driver flags --preprocess --compile --link
  • One ODR fix documented
  • -Wall -Wextra clean
  • ≥12 API tests
Done when: Clean rebuild; driver explains each artifact.
Q52 MEGA
Header-Only Math Library With Single Entry
Your goal:

Practice ODR-safe inline design.

What you must do:

Ship mathlib.hpp as single include with vector ops, constants, version macros, examples/, tests/.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • #pragma once
  • inline/constexpr only
  • ≥15 constexpr tests
  • No .cpp required
Done when: Consumer includes one file and passes tests.
Q53 MEGA
Include Dependency Audit Tool
Your goal:

Reduce compile times by fixing include bloat.

What you must do:

Scan #includes, build dependency graph, flag unused includes by heuristic.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Graph DOT or JSON
  • ≥8 files
  • Forward-decl suggestions
  • Document false positives
Done when: One removal drops -E line count.
Q54 MEGA
Cross-Platform Path and Macro Facade
Your goal:

Portable preprocessor without #ifdef soup.

What you must do:

platform.hpp for PATH_SEP, export macros, GCC/Clang/MSVC detection.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • WIN32 vs linux
  • static_assert unknown platform
  • Demo main
  • ≥10 macro tests
Done when: Same sources on two toolchains.
Q55 MEGA
Symbol Visibility and Link Map Lab

See linker exports from your TUs.

What you must do:

Shared and static API variants; compare exports with hidden/default visibility.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

  • Hidden internals

  • ≥8 tests
Done when: Internal helpers not exported.
Q56 MEGA
compile_commands.json Generator

Parse manifest; emit compile_commands.json for 6+ files.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

Done when: clangd navigates headers.
Q57 MEGA
Unity Build vs Separate TU Benchmark

Done when: Non-zero on injected bug.

Q60 MEGA

Done when: Consume in fresh dir via README.

02 — Fundamental Types, const & auto

Topic Map: Fundamentals → Sizes → const → auto/decltype → constexpr → Conversions → Attributes

1. Fundamental types and memory

Integers, floats, and char have implementation-defined sizes. Use sizeof and <limits>.

std::cout << sizeof(int) << " " << sizeof(long) << " " << sizeof(double);
static_assert(sizeof(void*) == 8, "examples assume 64-bit");
TypeTypical roleNotes
boolConditionsPromotes to int in arithmetic
charBytes / narrow textSignedness implementation-defined
size_tSizes, indicesUnsigned; pairs with .size()

2. const correctness

void print(const std::string& s);
class Blob {
    int value() const { return n_; }
    mutable int cache_hits_{0};  // logical const
};
const int* p = &x;       // can't change *p
int* const q = &x;        // can't change q
const int* const r = &x;

3. auto and decltype

auto i = 42;
auto& ref = i;
decltype((i)) d = i;  // int& — parens keep references!
auto [a, b] = std::pair{1, 2};
Trap: auto x = {1, 2, 3}; deduces std::initializer_list, not vector.

4. constexpr and compile-time work

constexpr int sq(int n) { return n * n; }
constexpr int kMax = sq(10);
consteval int must_ct(int n) { return n * 2; }  // C++20

5. Safe conversions

enum class Color : uint8_t { Red, Green, Blue };
int x{3};      // OK
// int y{3.14}; // error: narrowing
Memory lens: Pick the smallest type that fits when layout matters; watch padding with alignas.
Types are contracts: const is a promise the compiler enforces. auto deduces — write types when it clarifies intent.

Common blockers

  • signed overflow → UB; use unsigned or wider type when wrap is intended.
  • auto braced init → auto x{1,2} is initializer_list, not pair.

Connects forward

→ Operators uses promotion rules. → const methods in classes.

Key Takeaways:
  1. const on parameters and member functions documents read-only intent and enables use on const objects.
  2. auto deduces from initializers; braced init can yield initializer_list.
  3. decltype((expr)) preserves references; bare decltype(expr) does not.
  4. Use enum class for type-safe enumerations without implicit int conversion.
  5. Brace initialization T{val} rejects narrowing conversions at compile time.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-fundamentals

Q1 HARD
Print sizeof table for all fundamental types on your platform.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in types-const — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Print sizeof table for all fundamental types on your platform. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

  • ≥15 tests
  • ostream<<
  • Demo fails on mix

Layout plus static_assert.

Packed packets; const parsers; endian swap.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • static_assert offsets
  • ≥10 tests
  • Document packing
  • Swap helpers
Done when: Bad layout fails assert.

Your goal:

Compile-time tables.

What you must do:

CRC32 table; constexpr hash; switch on string_view.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No runtime init
  • ≥8 constexpr tests
  • 5 commands
  • Collision note
Done when: C++20 tests compile.

Done when: constexpr combos work.

Q59 MEGA

if constexpr serialization.

What you must do:

serialize(T) for integral, float, enum, string.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

  • ≥15 tests

Done when: Each branch tested.
Q60 MEGA
Capstone: JSON Value Tree With variant
Your goal:

Cap types with nested variant tree.

What you must do:

MEGA: Parse/print JSON subset; deep const traversal; no external libs.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Parse + print
  • ≥25 tests
  • const API
  • Value category docs
Done when: Round-trip fixtures.

03 — Operators, Control Flow & Functions

Topic Map: Operators → Precedence → Control flow → Functions → Overloading → constexpr if

1. Operators and precedence

C++ has rich operator overloading later — first master built-in precedence: arithmetic → shifts → comparisons → logical → assignment.

int a = 1 + 2 * 3;       // 7, not 9
bool ok = (a > 0 && a < 10);
int bits = (1 << 4) | 0x0F;

1.1 Short-circuit

if (ptr && ptr->valid()) { /* safe */ }
for (int i = 0; i < n && data[i] != target; ++i) { }

2. Control flow

switch (cmd) {
    case 'q': return 0;
    case 'h': help(); break;
    default:  std::cerr << "unknown\n";
}
for (const auto& x : items) { process(x); }
while (std::getline(in, line)) { parse(line); }
Switch trap: Missing break falls through — sometimes intentional, often a bug. Use [[fallthrough]] when deliberate.

3. Functions — value, reference, pointer

void bump(int& x) { ++x; }           // modifies caller
void log(const std::string& msg);    // read-only, no copy
int* find(int* begin, int* end, int v);  // half-open range

// Default args — rightmost only
void connect(std::string host, int port = 443);

4. Overloading and templates preview

int max(int a, int b) { return a > b ? a : b; }
double max(double a, double b) { return a > b ? a : b; }
// Later: template<typename T> T max(T a, T b);

5. constexpr if (C++17)

template<typename T>
auto get_value(T& t) {
    if constexpr (std::is_pointer_v<T>)
        return *t;
    else
        return t;
}
Ownership: Pass large objects by const&; return small POD by value (often elided). Out-parameters via pointer only when C API interop demands it.
Operators are function calls: a + b may call operator+. Overload resolution picks the best match — learn to read compiler errors.

Connects forward

→ Pointers for parameter passing. → Operator overloading on classes later.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Short-circuit && and || skip right-hand evaluation — use for null checks before dereference.
  2. Range-based for copies unless you take const auto& or auto& deliberately.
  3. Pass read-only inputs as const& to avoid copies; use non-const & for in-out parameters.
  4. Function overloading resolves at compile time by best match — ambiguities are compile errors.
  5. if constexpr discards non-taken branches at compile time — essential for generic code.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-types-const

Q1 HARD
Implement expression evaluator supporting +,-,*,/,% with correct precedence and ...
Your goal:

Master core C++ in operators-flow — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement expression evaluator supporting +,-,*,/,% with correct precedence and parentheses. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.
Q2 HARD
Trace short-circuit behavior: log which side of && and || evaluated.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in operators-flow — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Analyze and predict exact behavior for: Trace short-circuit behavior: log which side of && and || evaluated For each case write predicted output/UB/leak, then verify with compiler + sanitizer. Explain the underlying C++ rule (lifetime, ODR, overload, etc.) in 2–4 sentences each.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Written predictions match compiler/sanitizer output; no hand-waving.

Q27 BRUTAL
Logical vs bitwise AND/OR
Your goal:

Master core C++ in operators-flow — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Logical vs bitwise AND/OR — print truth table for 0 and 1. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

04 — Pointers, References & nullptr

Topic Map: Address → Dereference → nullptr → References → const pointers → Invalidation

1. Pointers hold addresses

int n = 10;
int* p = &n;     // address-of
*p = 20;          // dereference — writes through pointer
assert(n == 20);
int* q = nullptr; // always prefer over NULL or 0

2. References — non-null aliases

Must bind at initialization; cannot reseat. Use for parameters you won't rebind.

void swap_refs(int& a, int& b) {
    int tmp = a; a = b; b = tmp;
}
// int& r;  // error: must initialize

3. const pointer combinations

const int* ro = &n;    // read-only view
int* const pinned = &n;  // fixed address
const int* const frozen = &n;

3.1 Read it aloud

const int* = pointer to const int. int* const = const pointer to int.

4. Array decay and pointer arithmetic

int arr[5] = {1,2,3,4,5};
int* it = arr;           // decays to int*
int* end = arr + 5;      // one-past-end — valid for [begin,end)
for (; it != end; ++it) std::cout << *it;
UB zone: Dereferencing past one-past-end, returning pointer to local stack variable, double-delete — draw the lifetime diagram first.

5. When references beat pointers

  • Parameter that must exist → reference
  • Optional or reseatable → pointer + nullptr check
  • Non-owning observer → raw pointer or reference (document lifetime!)
struct Node { int val; Node* next; };  // non-owning link — who owns nodes?
Feel it: Every btn every raw pointer ask: Who allocates? Who frees? Can it be null? Can the pointee outlive the pointer?
Memory feel: Pointer = address + promise you know the object lifetime. Reference = non-null alias. Draw boxes and arrows.

Common blockers

  • Dangling reference → never bind to temporaries or expired locals.
  • vector::data() → invalid after reallocation.

Connects forward

→ RAII owns heap memory. → Iterators generalize pointers.

Key Takeaways:
  1. nullptr is type-safe; never use NULL or bare 0 for pointers.
  2. References must bind to valid objects and cannot be reseated — use for required parameters.
  3. Pointer arithmetic is only defined within the same array object (including one-past-end).
  4. Returning address of a local variable creates dangling pointers — UB on use.
  5. Draw ownership: raw pointers for observe/non-own by convention; owners use RAII (next topic).

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-operators-flow

Q1 HARD
Draw memory diagram: int on stack, pointer on stack pointing to it.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in pointers — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Draw memory diagram: int on stack, pointer on stack pointing to it. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Solve this challenge completely: Deep copy linked list with raw new per node. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

05 — Stack, Heap, Lifetime & RAII

Topic Map: Stack vs heap → Lifetime → new/delete → RAII → Exception safety → Rule of zero preview

1. Storage duration

DurationAllocationEnds when
Automatic (stack)Compiler in frameScope exits
Dynamic (heap)new / allocatordelete or leak
StaticProgram startupProgram exit
void f() {
    int x = 42;              // stack — automatic cleanup
    int* p = new int{42};   // heap — YOU must delete
    delete p;
}

2. RAII — Resource Acquisition Is Initialization

Bind resource lifetime to object lifetime. Destructor runs on scope exit — even during stack unwinding from exceptions.

struct FileGuard {
    FILE* f{nullptr};
    explicit FileGuard(const char* path) : f(std::fopen(path, "r")) {}
    ~FileGuard() { if (f) std::fclose(f); }
    FileGuard(const FileGuard&) = delete;
    FileGuard& operator=(const FileGuard&) = delete;
};

3. new/delete vs new[]/delete[]

int* one = new int{5};
delete one;

int* many = new int[100];
delete[] many;  // mismatch → UB, often caught by ASan
Feel it: Every naked new should hurt until you know the owner. Prefer stack objects, containers, and (later) smart pointers.

4. Exception safety basics

If ctor throws after partial setup, only fully constructed subobjects get destroyed. Never let raw owning pointers leak on exception paths.

void risky() {
    FileGuard g("data.txt");  // fclose guaranteed
    std::vector<int> v(1'000'000);  // frees on throw
    throw std::runtime_error("fail");  // g and v still destroyed
}

5. Copy elision and RVO

std::vector<int> make() {
    return std::vector<int>{1, 2, 3};  // often no copy — NRVO
}
Checklist: (1) Who owns this memory? (2) What runs on every exit path? (3) Can I use a guard class instead of manual cleanup?
RAII is the spine: Constructor acquires, destructor releases. Stack unwinding calls dtors — your safety net when exceptions throw.
Blocker killer: Every new needs a named owner who deletes on every path.

Connects forward

→ Rule of Five. → Smart pointers automate ownership.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Stack objects die at scope end; heap objects live until explicitly freed or leaked.
  2. RAII ties cleanup to destructors — the foundation of exception-safe C++.
  3. delete vs delete[] must match how memory was allocated.
  4. Partial construction + exceptions require members (not manual steps) to manage resources.
  5. Return-by-value with RVO often avoids heap allocation entirely — measure before micro-optimizing.

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-pointers

Q8 HARD
alignof/max_align_t and over-aligned type on heap.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in memory-raii — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Extend prior topic capstone code — do not rewrite from zero; prior tests must still pass.

Done when: Prior capstone tests still pass AND new feature has ≥5 new tests.

06 — Arrays, vector & string

Topic Map: C arrays → vector → string → SSO → Iterators → Invalidation

1. C arrays — fixed size, decay to pointer

int a[3] = {1, 2, 3};
// int b[n];  // VLAs not in standard C++ — use vector
void print(const int* begin, std::size_t len);  // decay loses size
Size loss: Arrays passed to functions decay — pass std::span (C++20) or vector& to keep length.

2. std::vector — dynamic array you should default to

std::vector<int> v{1, 2, 3};
v.reserve(1024);        // avoid realloc in hot loop
v.push_back(4);
v.emplace_back(5);      // construct in place
for (int x : v) { }

2.1 Invalidation

push_back may reallocate → all iterators and pointers/references into elements invalidate.

3. std::string — owning char sequence

std::string s = "hello";
s += " world";
std::string_view sv = s;  // non-owning view — watch lifetime!
auto sub = s.substr(0, 5);

Many implementations use SSO (small string optimization): short strings live inside the object, no heap.

4. Iterators as pointer generalization

auto it = v.begin();
auto end = v.end();
std::sort(v.begin(), v.end());
// end is one-past-last — same half-open convention as pointers

5. Ownership diagram for collections

std::vector<std::string> names;
names.emplace_back("Ada");  // vector owns string objects
// string_view into names[i] invalid after push_back that reallocates
Practice: Before holding &v[i] across mutations, check if reallocation can happen — or index by value after.
Sequences own layout: C array = size lost on decay. std::vector = size + capacity. std::string may use SSO — still value type.

Connects forward

→ Classes wrap invariants. → STL algorithms operate on ranges.

Key Takeaways:
  1. C arrays decay to pointers — size information is lost unless passed separately or wrapped.
  2. std::vector owns contiguous elements; reserve before bulk insert.
  3. Reallocation invalidates pointers/references/iterators into vector elements.
  4. std::string_view does not extend lifetime — underlying string must outlive the view.
  5. Prefer vector and string over manual new[] — they implement RAII for you.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-memory-raii

Q1 HARD
C array size lost on decay
Your goal:

Master core C++ in arrays-strings — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: C array size lost on decay — pass span or vector instead. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.

Done when: Stats match reference.

07 — Classes, Encapsulation & this

Topic Map: class vs struct → Encapsulation → this → const methods → static members → Access control

1. class vs struct

Only default access differs: class → private, struct → public. Use struct for passive data aggregates, class for invariants.

class BankAccount {
    double balance_{0.0};  // invariant: never negative
public:
    void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount <= 0) throw std::invalid_argument("deposit");
        balance_ += amount;
    }
    double balance() const { return balance_; }
};

2. this pointer and method calls

struct Counter {
    int n{0};
    Counter& inc() { ++n; return *this; }  // chaining
};
Counter c; c.inc().inc();

3. const correctness on methods

class Widget {
    std::string name_;
    mutable std::size_t reads_{0};
public:
    const std::string& name() const {
        ++reads_;
        return name_;
    }
    void rename(std::string n) { name_ = std::move(n); }
};

4. static members — one per class, not per instance

struct IdGen {
    static int next() { return ++counter_; }
private:
    static inline int counter_{0};
};

5. Member initialization order

Members initialize in declaration order, not initializer-list order — mismatches cause subtle bugs.

struct Bad {
    int a;
    int b;
    Bad(int v) : b(v), a(b) {}  // BUG: a uses uninitialized b
};
Encapsulation point: Public data members forfeits invariants. Keep representation private; expose operations that preserve rules.
Class = invariant + interface: Public API, private state. this is the current object address — use for chaining and const-correctness.

Connects forward

→ Constructors establish invariants. → Inheritance extends interfaces.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Default access: class private, struct public — choose by intent, not syntax habit.
  2. const member functions promise not to modify observable state (except mutable).
  3. Member initialization follows declaration order in the class body — not initializer-list order.
  4. this is a pointer to the current object — use for chaining or passing self to APIs.
  5. Static members belong to the class; define static data members in exactly one .cpp (unless inline).

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-arrays-strings

Q1 HARD
BankAccount class enforcing non-negative balance invariant.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in classes — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: BankAccount class enforcing non-negative balance invariant. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.

const object may only call const methods
Your goal:

Master core C++ in classes — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: const object may only call const methods — compile error demo. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Q31 HARD
Implement Cartesian Point class with distance and translate methods.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in classes — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement Cartesian Point class with distance and translate methods. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.

Register derived types.

map<string, factory> auto-register macros; discuss SIOF.

Entity with typed component map; add/get/has.

on/off/emit; lifetime of callbacks documented.

  • ≥15 tests
  • Table driven
  • Diagram in README
  • Current state query
Done when: Invalid transition throws.
Q58 MEGA
Inventory SKU Encapsulation
Your goal:

Business invariants.

What you must do:

Item SKU, qty; reserve, sell; no negative stock.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥12 tests
  • Invariant comments
  • const query methods
  • Exception on violate
Done when: Cannot oversell.

What you must do:

Levels, context push/pop, formatted lines.

  • RAII context guard
Done when: Context appears in line.
Q60 MEGA
Capstone: Game Module With Entity + Components
Your goal:

MEGA class design.

What you must do:

MEGA: Entities, components, systems sketch; factory; tests; no globals.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥25 tests
  • pimpl optional
  • Encapsulation review
  • ASan
  • Architecture README
Done when: Demo spawns and updates entities.

08 — Constructors, Destructors & Rule of Five

Topic Map: Constructors → Delegation → Destructors → Rule of Three/Five/Zero → Explicit → deleted functions

1. Constructors establish invariants

class Buffer {
    std::size_t len_{0};
    int* data_{nullptr};
public:
    explicit Buffer(std::size_t n)
        : len_(n), data_(new int[n]) {}
    ~Buffer() { delete[] data_; }
};

2. Member initializer lists — required for refs and const

class Employee {
    const int id_;
    std::string& dept_;  // must refer to valid string
public:
    Employee(int id, std::string& d) : id_(id), dept_(d) {}
};

3. Rule of Five

If you define or delete any of: destructor, copy ctor, copy assign, move ctor, move assign — consider all five.

Buffer(const Buffer& o) : len_(o.len_), data_(new int[o.len_]) {
    std::copy(o.data_, o.data_ + len_, data_);
}
Buffer& operator=(const Buffer& o) {
    if (this == &o) return *this;
    delete[] data_;
    len_ = o.len_;
    data_ = new int[len_];
    std::copy(o.data_, o.data_ + len_, data_);
    return *this;
}
Buffer(Buffer&& o) noexcept : len_(o.len_), data_(o.data_) {
    o.data_ = nullptr; o.len_ = 0;
}

4. Rule of Zero — prefer

class Team {
    std::string name_;
    std::vector<int> scores_;
    // compiler-generated special members are correct
};
Default: Let vector, string, smart pointers manage memory — write zero manual special members.

5. explicit and deleted functions

class Meter {
    explicit Meter(double v) : v_(v) {}  // block implicit conversion
    int v_;
};
struct NonCopyable {
    NonCopyable() = default;
    NonCopyable(const NonCopyable&) = delete;
};
Self-assignment: Copy assignment must handle *this == other and provide strong guarantee or document basic guarantee.
Rule of Five: If you manage a resource (raw pointer, file handle), define or delete copy/move/dtor together. Rule of Zero when possible.
Copy = duplicate ownership unless shared intentionally. Move = transfer ownership, leave source valid-but-empty.

Connects forward

→ Polymorphism needs virtual dtor. → Move semantics refines transfer.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Constructors run in member declaration order; base classes before members.
  2. If you manage raw resources, implement or delete Rule-of-Five operations explicitly.
  3. Rule of Zero: compose RAII types so compiler-generated special members suffice.
  4. explicit on single-arg ctors prevents silent implicit conversions.
  5. Destruction is automatic on scope exit — order is reverse of construction.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-classes

Q1 HARD
Default ctor, value ctor, copy ctor
Your goal:

Master core C++ in ctors-dtor — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Default ctor, value ctor, copy ctor — label which compiler generates. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Your goal:

Master core C++ in ctors-dtor — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

09 — Inheritance, virtual & Polymorphism

Topic Map: Base/derived → Access → virtual → Override → slicing → Polymorphism → vtable

1. Inheritance models is-a — use carefully

struct Shape {
    virtual ~Shape() = default;  // critical for delete via base*
    virtual double area() const = 0;  // pure virtual — abstract
};
struct Circle : Shape {
    double r;
    double area() const override { return 3.14159 * r * r; }
};

2. virtual dispatch

Circle c{5.0};
Shape* p = &c;
std::cout << p->area();  // Circle::area — dynamic dispatch

2.1 Without virtual

Base pointer calls base method — silent logic bug, not compile error.

3. Object slicing

Circle c{3.0};
Shape s = c;  // slices — only Shape subobject copied
// s.area() — wrong type, likely abstract call error if pure
Always polymorphic base needs virtual destructor when deleted through base pointer.

4. override and final (C++11)

struct B { virtual void f(); };
struct D : B { void f() override; };  // compiler checks base exists
struct E final : D { void f() final; };

5. Access and hiding

struct Base { virtual void g(); void h(); };
struct Der : Base {
    void g() override;   // overrides virtual
    void h();            // hides Base::h — not override!
};
// p->Base::h() to call base version

6. vtable intuition

Polymorphic classes carry a vptr; virtual calls indirect through vtable. Cost: one pointer per object + indirect branch — pay only when you need runtime polymorphism.

Design: Prefer composition over inheritance. Use inheritance for Liskov substitutability — derived must honor base contracts.
Polymorphism costs a vtable: virtual enables runtime dispatch. Prefer composition when you don't need runtime substitution.

Common blockers

  • Missing virtual dtor → UB deleting derived via base pointer.
  • Object slicing → pass by reference or pointer, not by value.

Connects forward

→ STL uses templates for static polymorphism. → unique_ptr<Base> patterns.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Polymorphic base classes need virtual ~Base() when objects are deleted via base pointer.
  2. override catches signature mismatches; without virtual you get static binding.
  3. Object slicing copies only the base subobject — avoid value-typed polymorphism.
  4. Pure virtual functions (= 0) make classes abstract — cannot instantiate directly.
  5. Non-virtual functions in derived classes hide base names — use using Base::h; or qualify calls.

10 — STL Containers & Iterators

Topic Map: Sequence → Associative → Unordered → Adaptors → Iterators → Invalidation

1. Sequence containers

std::vector stores elements contiguously — cache-friendly, amortized O(1) push_back. std::deque uses chunked blocks — O(1) push at both ends without invalidating other elements' addresses (but not contiguous). std::list and std::forward_list are doubly/singly linked — stable iterators on insert/erase, poor locality.

std::vector<int> v{1, 2, 3};
v.reserve(1024);           // one allocation before hot loop
v.emplace_back(4);         // construct in place — no extra move
std::deque<int> dq;
dq.push_front(0);
dq.push_back(5);

2. Associative containers

std::map / std::set keep keys sorted in a red-black tree — O(log n) insert/find/erase. Iterators traverse in sorted order. multimap/multiset allow duplicate keys.

std::map<std::string, int> freq;
freq["alpha"]++;
auto it = freq.find("beta");
if (it != freq.end()) { /* found */ }

3. Unordered associative

Hash tables — average O(1) if hash spreads keys. Requires std::hash<Key> and operator==. Call reserve(n) before bulk insert to avoid rehash storms.

struct Point { int x, y; bool operator==(const Point&) const = default; };
struct PointHash {
    size_t operator()(Point p) const {
        return std::hash<int>{}(p.x) ^ (std::hash<int>{}(p.y) << 1);
    }
};
std::unordered_map<Point, int, PointHash> grid;

4. Iterator categories

Random access (vector), bidirectional (list, map), forward (forward_list), input/output. Algorithms require minimum category — e.g. std::sort needs random access.

5. Invalidation rules (memorize)

  • vector: insert/erase/realloc may invalidate all iterators and references.
  • deque: insert/erase invalidates iterators; references may survive except to erased element.
  • list/forward_list: only iterators to erased nodes invalidate.
  • map/set: only erased element's iterator invalidates.
  • unordered_*: rehash invalidates all; erase only erased element.
Feel it: Draw the container before holding a pointer/reference across push_back. Vector reallocation is the #1 STL footgun.

6. Adaptors

std::stack, std::queue, std::priority_queue wrap a underlying container (default deque/vector) with restricted interface — no iterators.

Pick container for operations: vector for cache-friendly iteration; map for ordered keys; unordered_map for average O(1); deque for both ends.
Invalidation: Know which operations invalidate iterators — the #1 STL bug in interviews and production.

Connects forward

→ Algorithms work on iterator ranges. → Move avoids copies on insert.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Q51 MEGA
Intrusive LRU Cache With Node Handles
Your goal:

Master list+hash invalidation rules and O(1) cache semantics.

What you must do:

Build LRUCache with get/put O(1) using std::list for order and std::unordered_map for lookup. PHASE 1: Node struct and splice-to-front on access. PHASE 2: Eviction from list tail on capacity. PHASE 3: C++17 node_handle extract/insert for key updates without reallocation of mapped nodes. PHASE 4: Statistics hits/misses/evictions. PHASE 5: Serialize cache state to JSON text.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ASan clean
  • ≥20 unit tests including capacity=1 edge
  • Benchmark vs naive map+timestamp
  • Document iterator stability on splice
Done when: Cache matches manual trace on 50-operation script; node_handle path tested separately.
Q52 MEGA
Sorted Index Engine (Inverted Index Core)
Your goal:

Combine map, unordered_map, vector, and algorithm iterators.

What you must do:

Implement inverted index: tokenize documents, store doc_id->positions in unordered_map<string, vector<int>> or map for ordered traversal. Support AND/OR queries, phrase search with position lists intersection. Load 100+ docs from corpus directory.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Tokenizer handles punctuation/UTF-8 ASCII subset
  • Query parser for simple boolean expressions
  • ≥25 tests on synthetic corpus
  • CLI search REPL
Done when: Query 'foo AND bar' returns correct doc ids ranked by tf-idf stub.
Q53 MEGA
Graph Library With Pluggable Storage
Your goal:

Choose container representation for algorithm performance.

What you must do:

Graph class with adjacency list (vector<vector<int>>), edge list, and optional unordered_map for sparse string nodes. Implement BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, topo sort. Switch storage backend at compile time via template parameter.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Directed/undirected mode
  • Dijkstra with unreachable = inf
  • Import/export GraphML-like JSON
  • 22+ tests
Done when: Shortest path and cycle detection correct on 5 fixture graphs.
Q54 MEGA
Flat Hash Map From Scratch (Robin Hood)
Your goal:

Understand unordered_map internals and hash policy.

What you must do:

Implement open-addressing hash map with linear probing and optional robin-hood displacement. Compare to std::unordered_map on insert/find/delete 1e6 keys. Support reserve/load_factor API.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No std::unordered_map in implementation
  • Custom hash for int/string keys
  • Collision count metrics printed
  • 15+ tests
Done when: Correctness matches STL on fuzz 10k random ops; within 2x STL perf acceptable.
Q55 MEGA
Small Vector Optimization Library
Your goal:

Sequence container memory and invalidation mastery.

What you must do:

SmallVector<T,N> storing N elements inline then heap. Implement push_back, pop_back, reserve, iterators, move. Benchmark vs std::vector for small counts.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • noexcept move if T allows
  • Random access iterators
  • ASan + exception safety on grow
  • 18+ tests
Done when: No heap alloc until size exceeds N; move leaves source empty.
Q56 MEGA
Interval Tree / Segment Tree on vector
Your goal:

Advanced contiguous storage patterns.

What you must do:

Build segment tree for range sum query and point update on vector-backed tree array. Optional interval tree for overlap queries on [l,r) intervals stored in ordered map.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • O(log n) query/update
  • Build from vector in O(n)
  • Visualize tree levels to ostream
  • 20+ tests
Done when: Range queries match naive on random tests.
Q57 MEGA
Priority Scheduler With Stable Ordering
Your goal:

Container adaptors and comparator design.

What you must do:

StablePriorityQueue using counter tie-breaker. Job scheduler processes jobs with priority/deadline; log wait time and lateness. Support decrease-key via hash map to heap index (optional hard mode).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • std::priority_queue or custom heap
  • Stable FIFO among equal priority
  • Scheduler simulation ≥1000 jobs
  • Heapify O(n) build
Done when: Equal-priority jobs finish FIFO; deadlines met on test set.
Q58 MEGA
Memory Pool Allocator for Fixed Nodes
Your goal:

Allocator awareness with vector/list containers.

What you must do:

Pool allocator for fixed-size nodes; use with list or custom vector. Benchmark graph allocation vs new/delete. Thread-safe optional extension documented.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ASan clean return-to-pool
  • O(1) allocate/free
  • Use pool with ≥1 STL container via allocator template param
  • Benchmark table
Done when: No leaks; measurable speedup on 1e6 node churn.
Q59 MEGA
Consistent Hashing Ring
Your goal:

Ordered map for ring topology.

What you must do:

Implement consistent hash ring with std::map<uint32_t, Node> virtual nodes. Add/remove server reshards minimal keys. Simulate 1e5 key placements; measure variance.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Virtual node count configurable
  • SHA1 or FNV hash to uint32
  • CLI add/rm server commands
  • Document key migration count
Done when: Adding server moves only ~1/n keys empirically.
Q60 MEGA
STL Invalidation Laboratory
Your goal:

Empirical invalidation matrix for all major containers.

What you must do:

Write test program probing iterator/reference validity across insert/erase/rehash for vector/deque/list/map/unordered_map. Output markdown table generated from code. Include fix recipes per failure mode.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Automated probes not manual docs only
  • ≥30 distinct probe cases
  • Reproduce one UB with sanitizer then fix
  • README explains each row
Done when: Generated table matches standard invalidation rules; UB demo caught by ASan.

11 — STL Algorithms, Lambdas & Ranges

Topic Map: Iterators → Algorithm families → Lambdas → Ranges → Complexity

1. Iterator-based algorithms

STL algorithms are non-member functions operating on half-open ranges [first, last). They never allocate container memory — only read/write through iterators.

std::vector<int> v{3, 1, 4, 1, 5};
std::sort(v.begin(), v.end());
auto it = std::lower_bound(v.begin(), v.end(), 4);
bool found = std::binary_search(v.begin(), v.end(), 4);

2. Common families

  • Non-modifying: find, count, all_of, for_each
  • Modifying: transform, fill, generate, reverse
  • Sorting/partition: sort, stable_sort, nth_element, partition
  • Numeric: accumulate, inner_product, partial_sum (<numeric>)
  • Set ops (sorted ranges): set_union, set_intersection

3. Remove–erase idiom

std::remove does NOT shrink containers — it moves kept elements forward and returns new logical end. Must call erase on containers.

v.erase(std::remove(v.begin(), v.end(), 0), v.end());

4. Lambdas as predicates

std::sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int a, int b) { return a > b; });
auto n = std::count_if(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int x) { return x % 2 == 0; });

Capture [=], [&], or explicit — prefer explicit to avoid dangling references.

5. C++20 ranges

#include <ranges>
auto evens = v | std::views::filter([](int x){ return x % 2 == 0; })
               | std::views::transform([](int x){ return x * x; });

Lazy evaluation — no intermediate vector unless you materialize with std::ranges::to (C++23) or loop.

Algorithm choice: If data is sorted, binary search beats linear scan. If only top-k needed, nth_element beats full sort.
Algorithms + iterators = separation: Logic (sort, find) separate from storage (vector). remove + erase is one idiom — not optional.

Connects forward

→ Lambdas as custom predicates. → Ranges compose pipelines (C++20).

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-stl-containers

Master core C++ in stl-algorithms — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

Solve this challenge completely: Combine algorithms: copy_if -> sort -> unique -> erase for dedup pipeline. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Pedagogy through trace output.

Q56 MEGA

Q57 MEGA

Choose right algorithm for partial ordering.

Process 1e7 event stream; maintain top K=100 by score using partial_sort or nth_element each window. Compare heap alternative.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Memory O(K) or justify
  • Benchmark three approaches
  • Windowed tumbling semantics documented
  • Correctness on synthetic stream
Done when: Scores match full sort baseline on sample windows.
Q58 MEGA
Calendar Interval Merge Pipeline
Your goal:

Sort + merge + erase pipeline mastery.

What you must do:

Parse interval list; sort by start; merge overlapping; subtract blackout intervals; output free slots. Use only algorithm + vector tools.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):

  • ≥15 edge cases (touching, nested)

Done when: Merged intervals match manual on 20 fixtures.
Q59 MEGA

Done when: Same tests pass on both storage backends.
Q60 MEGA
Sort Comparator Audit Tool
Your goal:

Understand strict weak ordering requirements.

What you must do:

Given comparator lambda, property-test on random arrays for irreflexivity, asymmetry, transitivity. Detect bad comparators that hang std::sort.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Generate counterexample when bad
  • Works with user-provided comparator file
  • Explain failure mode
  • ≥8 known bad comparators detected
Done when: Detects <= vs < mistake comparator reliably.

12 — Value Categories, move & forward

Topic Map: Value categories → std::move → Rule of Five → Perfect forwarding

1. Value categories

lvalue — has identity, you can take address (int x;). rvalue — temporary or moved-from (42, result of std::move(x)). C++11 refines: glvalue, prvalue, xvalue.

int x = 10;
int& lr = x;           // lvalue reference
int&& rr = 42;         // rvalue reference binds to temporary
const int& cr = 42;    // const lvalue ref extends temporary lifetime

2. Move constructor & assignment

Steal resources from dying object; leave moved-from in valid empty state.

class Buffer {
    char* data_; size_t n_;
public:
    Buffer(Buffer&& other) noexcept
        : data_(other.data_), n_(other.n_) {
        other.data_ = nullptr;
        other.n_ = 0;
    }
    Buffer& operator=(Buffer&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {
            delete[] data_;
            data_ = other.data_; n_ = other.n_;
            other.data_ = nullptr; other.n_ = 0;
        }
        return *this;
    }
};

3. std::move is a cast

std::move(x) → static_cast<T&&>(x). Does not move by itself — enables overload resolution to pick move overload.

4. Copy elision & NRVO

Compiler may elide copies on return — mandatory in C++17 for prvalues. Do not std::move local return — can block NRVO.

5. Perfect forwarding

template <class T>
void wrapper(T&& arg) {
    foo(std::forward<T>(arg));  // preserve value category
}

std::forward + universal reference T&& in deduced context forwards lvalues as lvalues, rvalues as rvalues.

Rule of Five: If you define/copy/move/destroy manually, consider all five: destructor, copy ctor, copy assign, move ctor, move assign. Mark moves noexcept so vector reallocation uses move not copy.
Value categories: lvalue has identity; rvalue is expiring. Move pilfers resources — moved-from object must be valid but unspecified state.

Connects forward

→ Smart pointers use move. → Perfect forwarding with templates.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Answer thoroughly: Explain why std::vector<T> requires MoveAssignable — not just MoveConstructible. Minimal reproducible example, the standard rule, a common wrong fix, and when this matters in production C++.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Q60 MEGA
Forwarding Wrapper Library

Build utilities used by bridge and containers.

Implement forward_as_tuple, invoke, bind_front with perfect forwarding and value category tests.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Reference collapsing tests
  • No dangling bind to temporaries in tests
  • ≥18 unit tests
  • Header-only
Done when: invoke calls correct overload for member function pointers.

13 — unique_ptr, shared_ptr & weak_ptr

Topic Map: unique_ptr → shared_ptr → weak_ptr → Custom deleters → Ownership graphs

1. unique_ptr — exclusive ownership

Zero overhead vs raw pointer when not empty. Move-only. Use std::make_unique<T>(args) (C++14).

auto p = std::make_unique<Widget>(42);
// auto q = p;           // ERROR: copy deleted
auto q = std::move(p);   // ownership transfer
assert(!p);              // empty

2. shared_ptr — shared ownership

Reference-counted control block (separate from object). std::make_shared single allocation for object + control block.

auto a = std::make_shared<Node>();
auto b = a;              // use_count == 2
b.reset();               // use_count == 1

3. weak_ptr — break cycles

Non-owning observer. lock() returns shared_ptr or empty if object destroyed.

std::weak_ptr<Node> w = a;
if (auto sp = w.lock()) { /* object alive */ }

4. Custom deleters

auto f = std::unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(&fclose)>(
    fopen("data.txt", "r"), &fclose);

5. enable_shared_from_this

When object must hand out shared_ptr<self> from inside member function — inherit std::enable_shared_from_this<T>, call shared_from_this() only after managed by shared_ptr.

Default guidance: unique_ptr first. Upgrade to shared_ptr only when shared lifetime is required. Never mix owning raw new with smart pointers on same object.
Ownership vocabulary: unique_ptr = exclusive. shared_ptr = shared + control block cost. weak_ptr breaks cycles.

Connects forward

→ Factory returning unique_ptr. → enable_shared_from_this for async callbacks.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-move-semantics

  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.

Q51 MEGA
Object Pool With unique_ptr Recycle Deleter
Your goal:

Custom deleter as ownership policy.

What you must do:

Pool returns unique_ptr<T, PoolDeleter>; on destroy return slot to pool. Benchmark 1e6 acquire/release vs new/delete.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Fixed pool size + overflow policy
  • Thread-safe optional doc
  • ASan clean
  • ≥20 tests
Done when: Same address reused across cycles; no leaks.
Q52 MEGA
Async Resource Loader With shared_ptr Cache
Your goal:

shared_ptr + weak_ptr cache pattern.

What you must do:

Load heavy Resource from disk keyed by path; cache shared_ptr; weak_ptr for observers; evict LRU when cache full.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Double-load coalescing with mutex
  • ≥15 tests concurrent get
  • Document aliasing if any
  • Load failure exception path
Done when: Concurrent requests for same path load once.
Q53 MEGA
Intrusive Ptr + shared_ptr Interop
Your goal:

Understand control block alternatives.

What you must do:

intrusive_ptr with embeded refcount; convert to shared_ptr via custom deleter bridge for API export.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No double delete
  • ≥12 tests conversion roundtrip
  • Explain memory layout
  • ASan
Done when: Interop matches shared_ptr lifetime.
Q54 MEGA
enable_shared_from_this Async Callbacks
Your goal:

Safe self references in async code.

What you must do:

Service inherits enable_shared_from_this; posts callbacks holding shared_ptr from shared_from_this. Cancel via weak_ptr token.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No UB stack construction
  • ≥10 async tests
  • Simulate destroy before callback
  • Document lifetime
Done when: Callback skipped if service destroyed.
Q55 MEGA
Debug Shared Ptr With Allocation Trace
Your goal:

Observe refcount and deleter behavior.

What you must do:

Wrap allocator logging ctor/dtor site; shared_ptr with debug deleter printing type and stack stub. Detect cycles manually.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Log on inc/dec optional macro
  • Cycle demo + weak fix
  • ≥8 scenarios
  • README debug workflow
Done when: Leak and cycle visible in logs.
Q56 MEGA
Move-Only Handle Manager API
Your goal:

Express ownership in public API.

What you must do:

HandleManager issuing move-only Handle<T> to File/Window resources; revoke invalidates weak handle token.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No copy of Handle
  • Revoke closes resource
  • ≥18 tests
  • Exception safe acquire
Done when: Use-after-revoke detected via error_code.
Q57 MEGA
Shared Mutex Config Snapshot
Your goal:

Readers with shared_ptr const snapshot.

What you must do:

Background thread updates Config building new object; atomic_store shared_ptr; readers never lock mutation path.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Readers wait-free after load
  • Writer excludes readers
  • ≥12 thread tests
  • TSan clean
Done when: Readers see consistent snapshot version.
Q58 MEGA
unique_ptr Incomplete Type Pimpl
Your goal:

Compilation firewall idiom.

What you must do:

Widget public class with unique_ptr<Impl> pimpl; ctor/dtor in .cpp; rule of zero/move discussion.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No raw new in public header
  • Move-only widget if impl move-only
  • ≥10 tests via public API
  • Fast rebuild when impl changes
Done when: Client includes widget.h only; impl details hidden.
Q59 MEGA
Shared Ownership Graph Without Cycles
Your goal:

Design ownership with weak edges.

What you must do:

Scene graph: nodes shared_ptr, parent holds weak_ptr to avoid cycle; traverse safely. Serialize tree.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cycle prevention test
  • ≥15 traversal tests
  • ASan
  • Document ownership diagram
Done when: Destroy root releases entire tree.
Q60 MEGA
Custom Aliasing shared_ptr For Subobjects
Your goal:

Advanced shared_ptr constructor.

What you must do:

Buffer shared_ptr with aliasing pointer to slice; lifetime tied to buffer. Use in parser token views.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Aliasing slice tests
  • No premature destroy
  • ≥10 tests
  • Explain control block
Done when: Slice valid while buffer alive.

14 — Templates, SFINAE & C++20 Concepts

Topic Map: Function/class templates → Specialization → SFINAE → Concepts → Variadics

1. Function templates

template <typename T>
T max(T a, T b) { return (a < b) ? b : a; }

auto m = max(3, 7);        // deduced int
auto s = max<double>(3, 7); // explicit

2. Class templates

template <typename T, typename Alloc = std::allocator<T>>
class vector { /* ... */ };

3. Template specialization

template <typename T> struct is_pointer { static constexpr bool value = false; };
template <typename T> struct is_pointer<T*> { static constexpr bool value = true; };

4. SFINAE

Substitution Failure Is Not An Error — invalid template instantiations removed from overload set.

template <typename T, typename = std::enable_if_t<std::is_integral_v<T>>>
void foo(T x) { /* integral only */ }

5. C++20 concepts

template <std::integral T>
T add(T a, T b) { return a + b; }

template <typename T>
concept Hashable = requires(T a) {
    { std::hash<T>{}(a) } -> std::convertible_to<std::size_t>;
};

Concepts give clear error messages vs deep SFINAE failures.

6. Variadic templates

template <typename... Args>
void log(Args&&... args) {
    (std::cout << ... << args) << '\n';  // C++17 fold
}
Templates are compile-time codegen: Errors look scary — read from the bottom up. C++20 concepts constrain templates readable.

Connects forward

→ Generic algorithms. → pybind11 binds templates to Python.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-smart-pointers

Q1 HARD
Write function template max(T,U)
Your goal:

Master core C++ in templates — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Write function template max(T,U) — SFINAE vs common_type for return. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.

  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.

Master core C++ in templates — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.

  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Print timing table for ≥3 input sizes; explain cache/allocator effects.

Solve this challenge completely: Use auto non-type template parameter for string literal length N in std::array<char,N>. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.
