Q51 MEGA
Compile-Time JSON Parser (constexpr subset)
Your goal:

Templates + constexpr control flow.

What you must do:

Parse limited JSON (object/array/string/number/bool/null) into constexpr-friendly tree using string_view at compile time where possible.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Parse errors with line/col
  • ≥20 constexpr tests
  • Runtime parse same code path
  • No third-party JSON lib
Done when: static_assert parsed value equals expected on fixture strings.
Q52 MEGA
Expression Template Matrix Library
Your goal:

Lazy template composition.

What you must do:

Matrix types with lazy operator+ and operator* via expression templates; evaluate to vector only on assign. Compare perf naive eager.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Correctness vs naive 100 random
  • No excessive temporaries in benchmark
  • Document lazy eval
  • ≥15 tests sizes
Done when: Chained ops faster than naive for large N.
Q53 MEGA
Type-Safe Physical Units Library
Your goal:

Concepts prevent unit errors.

What you must do:

Meters, Seconds, MetersPerSecond with concept-checked arithmetic; compile fail adding Meters to Seconds.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • C++20 concepts
  • ≥10 compile-fail static tests documented
  • Runtime conversion factors explicit
  • Example physics calc
Done when: Bad unit compile errors readable.
Q54 MEGA
Policy-Based Logger Template
Your goal:

Template template parameters.

What you must do:

Logger<Policy> where Policy::write(msg) varies Console/File/Syslog stub. No virtual in hot path.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥3 policies
  • Swap policy at compile time
  • Benchmark vs virtual logger
  • ≥12 tests
Done when: Same log calls, different policies zero virtual.
Q55 MEGA
Build Your Own type_traits Subset
Your goal:

Metaprogramming fundamentals.

What you must do:

Implement is_same, is_integral, remove_reference, enable_if, conditional_t using partial specialization.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Match std results on ≥20 types
  • No including <type_traits> in trait headers
  • Tests compare std
  • Document specialization pattern
Done when: All trait tests match std::.
Q56 MEGA
Concepts-Driven Graph Algorithms
Your goal:

Modern constraints replace SFINAE.

What you must do:

Template BFS requiring AdjacencyRange concept with begin/end on neighbors. Works vector and custom adjacency types.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Clear concept definitions
  • Readable errors when concept fails
  • ≥18 tests
  • Header-only
Done when: Fails compile with helpful message on bad graph type.
Q57 MEGA
Variant Visitor From Type List
Your goal:

Variadic templates + std::variant.

What you must do:

Generate overloaded visitor from type list applying function per alternative. Use for JSON-like Value variant.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Supports ≥5 alternative types
  • Visit noexcept where possible
  • ≥15 tests
  • No std::visit in generator impl optional
Done when: Visitor dispatches all alternatives correctly.
Q58 MEGA
constexpr Metaprogramming Math Table
Your goal:

Compile-time computation.

What you must do:

constexpr factorial, fib, prime check, lookup table generation at compile time embedded in std::array.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • static_assert on known values
  • Runtime use same constexpr functions
  • Generate 256-entry sin lookup table
  • Document compile time impact
Done when: Tables available at runtime with zero compute.
Q59 MEGA
Function Ref Small Callable Wrapper
Your goal:

Type erasure vs templates tradeoff.

What you must do:

FunctionRef<R(Args...)> non-owning callable ref; FixedFunction<SBO> owning small buffer like std::function improvement.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No heap when lambda small
  • Move-only or copy documented
  • ≥20 tests
  • Compare size to std::function
Done when: Small lambdas no allocation.
Q60 MEGA
Modular std-like Algorithm Header
Your goal:

Production template organization.

What you must do:

Split sort, binary_search, heap into separate headers with concept constraints; unity build optional; explicit instantiation for int/double in .cpp.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Include-what-you-use clean
  • Compile time measured before/after explicit inst
  • ≥25 tests
  • README architecture
Done when: Faster link on int/double users; tests pass.

15 — Threads, Mutexes & Atomics

Topic Map: Threads → Mutexes → Atomics → Condition variables → Memory model

1. std::thread

void work(int id) { std::cout << "t" << id << '\n'; }
std::thread t(work, 1);
t.join();   // or detach() — must do one

Pass by value or move into thread — references to locals dangle if thread outlives scope.

2. Mutex & lock guards

std::mutex mu;
std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mu);  // RAII unlock
// std::unique_lock for condition_variable — can unlock early

3. condition_variable

std::condition_variable cv;
std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lk(mu);
cv.wait(lk, [&]{ return ready; });  // always use predicate loop

4. Atomics

std::atomic<int> counter{0};
counter.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);

Default memory_order_seq_cst is safest; tighten only with understanding of happens-before.

5. async & futures

auto fut = std::async(std::launch::async, []{ return heavy(); });
int result = fut.get();
Data races are UB. Two threads accessing same non-atomic without synchronization — ThreadSanitizer catches these. Prefer RAII locks; never hold lock while doing I/O.
Concurrency is hard: Data races = UB. Use mutex, atomic, or message passing. Always test with ThreadSanitizer.

Connects forward

→ GIL release in Python bridge for CPU work.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-templates

Q1 HARD
Launch std::thread with lambda; join vs detach lifecycle rules.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in concurrency — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Launch std::thread with lambda; join vs detach lifecycle rules. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.

Q17 HARD

Your goal:

Master core C++ in concurrency — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement parallel for over vector indices with std::thread chunking. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Demonstrate data-race-free design; run under ThreadSanitizer when applicable.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Live traffic simulator with producers/consumers metrics dashboard CLI. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.

Q51 MEGA
Work-Stealing Thread Pool
Your goal:

Advanced scheduling and lock patterns.

What you must do:

Fixed workers with local deque; steal from others when idle. Accept std::function tasks; graceful shutdown.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • TSan clean
  • ≥20 tests stress
  • Benchmark vs naive single queue
  • Stealing statistics logged
Done when: Load balances skewed task durations.
Q52 MEGA
Parallel Matrix Multiply Tiling
Your goal:

CPU cache aware concurrent design.

What you must do:

Blocked matrix multiply with thread pool; param tile size; compare naive parallel i-j-k vs tiled.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Correctness vs serial
  • ≥3 matrix sizes timed
  • No false sharing on result matrix
  • Document tile tuning
Done when: Tiled beats naive at large N on multicore.
Q53 MEGA
Concurrent LRU Cache (TSan Clean)
Your goal:

Combine containers + sync mega topic.

What you must do:

Thread-safe LRU from prior container mega with shared_mutex or striped locks; hit rate metrics under parallel access.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Correctness sequential oracle
  • ≥16 thread tests
  • TSan CI script
  • Benchmark readers/writers mix
Done when: No races; hit rate within 5% of single-thread on read-heavy.
Q54 MEGA
Rate Limiter With Semaphores
Your goal:

Resource bounding in concurrent systems.

What you must do:

Token bucket or leaky bucket using counting_semaphore + condition_variable; HTTP-like request simulator.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Configurable rate/burst
  • ≥12 timing tests with mocked clock optional
  • Thread-safe
  • Metrics export
Done when: Never exceeds configured RPS within tolerance.
Q55 MEGA
Producer-Consumer Log Pipeline
Your goal:

Classic cv + queue pattern.

What you must do:

Multiple producers log to lock-free queue stub or mutex queue; consumer batch writes disk; backpressure when full.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No log loss under normal load
  • Graceful drop or block policy documented
  • Benchmark throughput
  • Shutdown drains queue
Done when: 1e6 messages processed without corruption.
Q56 MEGA
RCU Config Snapshot Lite
Your goal:

Read-mostly concurrency pattern.

What you must do:

Readers lock-free read atomic<shared_ptr<Config>>; writer builds new Config then atomic_store; document grace period.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • TSan/ASan clean
  • ≥10 reader threads
  • Writer pause measurement
  • Compare to shared_mutex
Done when: Readers never block on writer after publish.
Q57 MEGA
Live Traffic Simulator Dashboard
Your goal:

End-to-end concurrent system.

What you must do:

Simulate N producers M consumers with adjustable rates; CLI dashboard prints queue depth, latency percentiles, drops.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Deterministic seed mode
  • Run 60s soak test
  • Clean shutdown
  • README metrics glossary
Done when: Dashboard reflects simulated load changes.
Q58 MEGA
Async I/O Poll Thread Model
Your goal:

Separate I/O from worker compute.

What you must do:

One thread polls socket/file readiness stub (pipe or sleep simulation); pushes work to compute pool; results callback.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No busy spin default
  • ≥15 integration tests
  • Document thread roles
  • TSan clean
Done when: Compute threads scale independently of poll rate.
Q59 MEGA
Sync Primitive Benchmark Lab
Your goal:

Measure mutex vs spinlock vs atomic.

What you must do:

Microbench critical sections 10ns, 100ns, 1us, 1ms; plot overhead; recommend thresholds.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Pin threads optional doc
  • ≥4 primitive types
  • Repeat stable median
  • Written analysis
Done when: Clear recommendation table by critical section length.
Q60 MEGA
Cancellable jthread Worker Farm
Your goal:

C++20 stop_token usage.

What you must do:

jthread workers processing tasks until stop_requested; cooperative cancel mid-batch; join on scope exit.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • C++20
  • Cancel within 100ms on synthetic long task
  • ≥12 tests
  • No detach leaks
Done when: Stop stops workers; no hung threads.

16 — Exceptions, Streams & filesystem

Topic Map: Exceptions → Safety guarantees → iostream → filesystem

1. Exception basics

try {
    throw std::runtime_error("fail");
} catch (const std::exception& e) {
    std::cerr << e.what() << '\n';
}

Throw by value, catch by const reference. Never throw raw pointers.

2. Exception safety guarantees

  • Nothrow: never throws (move, swap, destructors ideally).
  • Strong: failure leaves state unchanged (copy-and-swap idiom).
  • Basic: invariants hold; may leak partial resources without RAII.

3. iostream hierarchy

istream, ostream, iostream. std::ifstream/ofstream for files. Always check fail()/good() or use exceptions via exceptions().

std::ifstream in("data.txt");
std::string line;
while (std::getline(in, line)) { process(line); }

4. string streams

std::ostringstream oss;
oss << 42 << ' ' << 3.14;
std::string s = oss.str();

5. std::filesystem (C++17)

namespace fs = std::filesystem;
for (auto& e : fs::directory_iterator(".")) {
    std::cout << e.path() << '\n';
}
RAII + exceptions: Stack unwinding runs destructors — smart pointers and lock guards release on throw. Raw new in try block without RAII leaks on throw.
Exception guarantees: Basic (no leak), strong (rollback), nothrow (destructor). RAII makes basic achievable.

Connects forward

→ fstream + filesystem for tooling projects.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-concurrency

Q1 HARD
Throw std::runtime_error from deep helper; catch at main with what() print.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in exceptions-io — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Throw std::runtime_error from deep helper; catch at main with what() print. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.
Q2 HARD
Implement function with basic exception guarantee using RAII guards.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in exceptions-io — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement function with basic exception guarantee using RAII guards. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.

Master core C++ in exceptions-io — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: iostream manipulator library for tables, progress bars, color codes. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.
Q50 BRUTAL
Compare error handling strategies: exceptions vs expected<T,E> design doc.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in exceptions-io — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Compare error handling strategies: exceptions vs expected<T,E> design doc. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Print timing table for ≥3 input sizes; explain cache/allocator effects.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.
Q51 MEGA
Transactional File Writer Commit Protocol
Your goal:

Strong guarantee on persistent writes.

What you must do:

Write temp file, fsync, atomic rename over target; crash simulator leaves old file intact. Rollback on validation failure.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cross-platform rename notes
  • ≥15 failure injection tests
  • Exception on disk full simulation
  • ASan clean
Done when: Never partial corrupt target file visible.
Q52 MEGA
Mini JSON Serializer With Exception Safety
Your goal:

IO + exception guarantees combined.

What you must do:

Serialize/deserialize JSON subset to DOM tree; throw parse_error with line/col; strong guarantee on DOM mutate rollback via copy-on-write or transaction log.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥25 parse tests malformed input
  • Roundtrip 10 fixtures
  • No leaks on throw path
  • Pretty print ostream
Done when: Malformed input throws; valid roundtrips.
Q53 MEGA
Log Rotation Library
Your goal:

filesystem + ofstream integration.

What you must do:

Rotating file logger by size and file count; compress old stub; thread-safe optional; exception on open fail.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Cross-platform paths
  • ≥12 rotation tests
  • Configure max_files/max_bytes
  • Human readable format
Done when: Rotation preserves total size bound.
Q54 MEGA
Config Loader TOML-Like Subset
Your goal:

Parse + validate + report errors.

What you must do:

Parse key=value and [section] headers; typed getters int/double/string/bool; throw ConfigError with key path context.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥20 invalid configs tested
  • Load from ifstream
  • Merge defaults
  • Document grammar
Done when: Errors cite line and key.
Q55 MEGA
Stream Cipher File Pipeline
Your goal:

Binary IO and RAII.

What you must do:

Encrypt/decrypt file with XOR or simple cipher; RAII handles; verify roundtrip; handle partial read/write errors.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Binary ifstream/ofstream
  • ≥10 file fixtures
  • Exception on short read
  • Benchmark MB/s
Done when: Decrypt(encrypt(f)) equals f.
Q56 MEGA
Exception-Safe Batch Directory Processor
Your goal:

Basic guarantee across many files.

What you must do:

Walk directory; transform each file; continue-on-error collecting vector<ErrorRecord>; summary at end.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • filesystem recursive iterator
  • ≥15 mixed good/bad files
  • No leak on mid-batch throw
  • Summary CSV output
Done when: All good files processed when some fail.
Q57 MEGA
iostream Table Formatter Library
Your goal:

Formatted human output.

What you must do:

Column alignment, headers, rules, optional color ANSI; stream manipulators for int/float/string columns.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Width aware Unicode ASCII
  • ≥10 formatting tests
  • Works with ostringstream
  • README examples
Done when: Tables align for varied column widths.
Q58 MEGA
Error Strategy Comparison Doc + Demos
Your goal:

Engineering judgment exceptions vs codes.

What you must do:

Same API implemented twice: throwing vs expected/error_code; compare call site ergonomics and perf microbench.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥3 API functions
  • Written ≥600 word comparison
  • Demo programs both styles
  • When-to-use decision tree
Done when: Clear team guideline output.
Q59 MEGA
Multi-Thread Log Sink
Your goal:

Exception-safe concurrent ostream.

What you must do:

Thread-safe logger writing to file via queue consumer; formatting with ostream; never throw from noexcept log macro path.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • TSan clean
  • ≥10 producer threads
  • Flush policy configurable
  • Macro LOG_INFO fmt-like
Done when: No garbled lines under concurrent logging.
Q60 MEGA
Tail-Follow and Rotating Read
Your goal:

Streaming IO patterns.

What you must do:

Follow growing log file like tail -f; handle rotation rename; reopen on inode change detection stub.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Sleep polling configurable
  • ≥8 scenario tests
  • Graceful stop flag
  • Document platform limits
Done when: Detects new lines after rotation.

17 — CMake, Debuggers & Sanitizers

Topic Map: CMake → Targets → Debuggers → Sanitizers → CI

1. Minimal CMake

cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.20)
project(demo CXX)
add_executable(app main.cpp utils.cpp)
target_compile_features(app PRIVATE cxx_std_20)
target_compile_options(app PRIVATE -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic)
cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug
cmake --build build
ctest --test-dir build

2. Modern target-based CMake

add_library(core STATIC core.cpp)
target_include_directories(core PUBLIC include)
target_link_libraries(app PRIVATE core GTest::gtest_main)

3. Debuggers

gdb ./app → break main, run, next, print var, backtrace. Compile with -g. LLDB on macOS/Clang.

4. AddressSanitizer

cmake -B build -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="-fsanitize=address -fno-omit-frame-pointer -g"
ASAN_OPTIONS=detect_leaks=1 ./build/app

Catches heap buffer overflow, use-after-free, leaks. UBSan for undefined behavior; TSan for data races.

5. compile_commands.json

cmake -B build -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON — feeds clangd/IDE accurate flags.

Build hygiene: Out-of-source builds only. Pin C++ standard per target. Warnings as errors in CI after baseline clean.
Tooling is part of mastery: CMake + sanitizers + debugger = how you trust C++ at scale. Build in Debug with ASan habitually.

Connects forward

→ pybind11 CMake targets. → Portfolio projects with CI.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-exceptions-io

Q1 HARD
Write CMakeLists.txt for executable with C++20 and -Wall -Wextra.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in build-tooling — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Write CMakeLists.txt for executable with C++20 and -Wall -Wextra. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • CMake project must configure and build out-of-source (`cmake -B build && cmake --build build`).
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Q33 BRUTAL
Implement sanitizer CI job failing on leak in test binary.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in build-tooling — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Implement sanitizer CI job failing on leak in test binary. List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Build with `-fsanitize=address,undefined` and show clean run.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Q39 CUMULATIVE
CMake-ify prior capstone project with tests target.
Your goal:

Master core C++ in build-tooling — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CUMULATIVE: CMake-ify prior capstone project with tests target. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Extend prior topic capstone code — do not rewrite from zero; prior tests must still pass.
  • CMake project must configure and build out-of-source (`cmake -B build && cmake --build build`).
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.

  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Build with `-fsanitize=address,undefined` and show clean run.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.
Q51 MEGA
Reusable CMake Package With Exported Targets
Your goal:

Modern CMake packaging.

What you must do:

Library mylib with alias mylib::mylib; install(EXPORT); consumer find_package uses targets; version compat SameMajorVersion.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Consumer project test in repo
  • Build tree + install tree
  • ≥10 tests via CTest
  • README consume snippet
Done when: External project links mylib::mylib cleanly.
Q52 MEGA
CI Pipeline GCC/Clang Matrix + Sanitizers
Your goal:

Production CI hygiene.

What you must do:

GitHub Actions or local script: Debug Release builds; ASan job; run tests; clang-tidy optional; artifacts on failure.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥2 compilers
  • Fail on test fail
  • ASan on separate job
  • Badge-ready log output
Done when: Green on clean repo; red on intentional leak test branch.
Q53 MEGA
Devcontainer Docker Toolchain Image
Your goal:

Reproducible dev environment.

What you must do:

Dockerfile with gcc/clang cmake ninja gdb valgrind sanitizers; devcontainer.json for VS Code; verify build sample project.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Non-root user
  • ccache optional
  • Document host mount
  • Build sample in CI
Done when: Fresh clone builds inside container.
Q54 MEGA
Benchmark Regression Harness
Your goal:

Catch perf regressions in CI.

What you must do:

Google Benchmark or chrono harness stores baseline json; compare PR within threshold; fail on regression >5%.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥3 benchmarks
  • Stored baseline file
  • Noise handling median of 5
  • README update baseline flow
Done when: Detects intentional slowdown commit.
Q55 MEGA
Cross-Platform Release Packager
Your goal:

Shipping binaries correctly.

What you must do:

Build Release; strip symbols optional; package zip with LICENSE README; Windows PDB separate; Linux tarball.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Windows + Linux doc steps
  • Version from git tag
  • Checksum file
  • Smoke test script
Done when: Unpack and run demo on clean VM.
Q56 MEGA
Static Analysis Gate Script
Your goal:

Aggregate quality tools.

What you must do:

Script runs cppcheck, clang-tidy, warnings-as-errors build; unified report json; exit non-zero on high severity.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Configurable severity threshold
  • Run on sample project with known issues
  • Document suppressions policy
  • CI integration
Done when: Catches seeded bug; clean on fixed.
Q57 MEGA
Monorepo Build Graph Generator
Your goal:

Understand dependency DAG.

What you must do:

5 libraries with dependencies; CMake targets; script emits mermaid DAG from cmake --graphviz=build graph.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • No cycles
  • Parallel build safe
  • ≥20 tests total
  • Graph matches manual
Done when: Graph accurate; incremental rebuild minimal.
Q58 MEGA
Sanitizer Cookbook Repository
Your goal:

Educational reproducers for tooling.

What you must do:

5 programs each triggering ASan UBSan TSan MSan doc-only where unavailable; README decode stacks; fix versions.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • One command build each
  • Document compiler flags
  • Before/after output
  • Platform notes
Done when: Student reproduces and fixes each bug class.
Q59 MEGA
PGO/LTO Experiment Workflow
Your goal:

Advanced optimization tooling.

What you must do:

Document profile-gen use run; rebuild with LTO; measure benchmark delta; when worth complexity.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥1 real benchmark
  • Profile data checked in or generated
  • Compare O2 vs O2+LTO vs PGO
  • Written conclusion
Done when: Measured gains or honest null result.
Q60 MEGA
Reproducible Build Checklist
Your goal:

Supply chain and debuggability.

What you must do:

Pin compiler version; SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH; deterministic flags; hash verify artifact; document in REPRODUCIBLE.md.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Two builds same hash or explain diff
  • SBOM stub optional
  • CI reproducibility job sketch
  • ≥5 checklist items automated
Done when: Repeatable artifact hash on same commit.

18 — pybind11, ctypes & Performance Bridge

Topic Map: pybind11 → GIL → NumPy buffers → ctypes → Packaging & perf

1. Why bridge Python and C++

Python for orchestration, UX, ML pipelines. C++ for hot loops, tight memory, systems APIs, deterministic latency. Profile first — bridge only proven bottlenecks.

2. pybind11 module skeleton

#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>
namespace py = pybind11;

int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

PYBIND11_MODULE(fastmath, m) {
    m.doc() = "fast math extension";
    m.def("add", &add, "Add two ints");
}
# setup.py / pip install -e .
import fastmath
assert fastmath.add(2, 3) == 5

3. Classes & STL containers

py::class_<Widget>(m, "Widget")
    .def(py::init<int>())
    .def("value", &Widget::value)
    .def("__repr__", [](const Widget& w){ return "Widget(" + std::to_string(w.value()) + ")"; });

m.def("sum_vec", [](const std::vector<int>& v) {
    return std::accumulate(v.begin(), v.end(), 0);
});

4. NumPy zero-copy buffers

py::array_t<double> buf = ...;
auto r = buf.unchecked<1>();  // fast 1D access — no GIL per element
for (py::ssize_t i = 0; i < r.shape(0); ++i) { r(i) *= 2.0; }

Document lifetime: NumPy array must outlive C++ view. Use py::gil_scoped_release around long CPU-only sections.

5. GIL rules

Python C-API and pybind11 calls need GIL. Release during heavy C++ compute; re-acquire before calling back into Python.

{
    py::gil_scoped_release release;
    heavy_cpp_kernel();  // parallel OK if no Python API
}

6. ctypes / extern "C" alternative

// libadd.so
extern "C" int add_c(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
import ctypes
lib = ctypes.CDLL("./libadd.so")
lib.add_c.argtypes = [ctypes.c_int, ctypes.c_int]
lib.add_c.restype = ctypes.c_int

No automatic type conversion — manual, fragile, but no compile step per Python version if ABI stable.

7. Packaging

scikit-build-core + CMake, or pybind11's PYBIND11_MODULE with setuptools. Ship wheels per platform. Pin compiler ABI (manylinux, MSVC runtime).

Deep bridge checklist: (1) Who owns memory? (2) GIL held or released? (3) Exceptions map to Python? (4) py::keep_alive<0,1> for parent-child lifetime? (5) Thread safety documented?
When to leave Python: Profile first. pybind11 for C++ APIs; keep orchestration in Python. Release GIL for long CPU kernels.

Prerequisites

Python topics 07+ and C++ topics 01–17 solid. Never return pointers to stack across the boundary.

Key Takeaways:
  1. Understand why C++ does this — not only syntax.
  2. Draw memory and ownership before writing code.
  3. Compile with warnings and run sanitizers early.

Practice Questions (60)

Q1–Q50 HARD/BRUTAL Q51–Q60 MEGA projects cumulative: cpp-build-tooling, Python concurrency

Q1 HARD
Build minimal pybind11 module exporting add(int,int) and pip install -e .
Your goal:

Master core C++ in python-bridge — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Build a complete solution for: Build minimal pybind11 module exporting add(int,int) and pip install -e . List invariants and ownership on paper, edge cases, then pseudocode, then code. Must compile warning-free at `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic` (or /W4 on MSVC).

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Implement from scratch — no copying STL internals unless the question allows std:: usage explicitly.
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Provide Python test script importing the extension and asserting correctness.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Your goal:

Master core C++ in python-bridge — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

Solve this challenge completely: Ship manylinux wheel stub doc with auditwheel repair notes. Before coding: invariants, ownership diagram, edge cases. After coding: manually trace two non-trivial examples including destructor order.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
Done when: Compiles clean, tests pass, another developer can build without asking questions.

Master core C++ in python-bridge — feel memory and ownership, not syntax alone.

What you must do:

MEGA PROJECT — CAPSTONE: Production bridge template: logging, errors, GIL policy, semver, changelog. Phases: (1) API/design on paper, (2) core implementation, (3) error paths, (4) tests + sanitizers, (5) demo. Multi-hour build — plan modules first.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Create a standalone `.cpp` (and headers if needed) under `practice_cpp/<topic>/`.
  • Provide a `CMakeLists.txt` or documented g++/clang++ compile line for your target.
  • Add at least 8–12 tests (GoogleTest, Catch2, or doctest) covering edge cases.
  • Include a small `main()` demo printing real output for manual verification.
  • Document time/space complexity and memory ownership in comments where relevant.
  • Expect 200+ lines — split into logical headers/sources before coding.
Done when: All phases done; ≥10 tests pass; ASan clean; demo runs; logical header/source split.
Q51 MEGA
Production pybind11 Package Template
Your goal:

End-to-end bridge ready for team use.

What you must do:

C++ core library + pybind11 module + scikit-build-core/pyproject.toml + pytest + GitHub Actions wheels stub. Semantic versioning, changelog, GIL policy doc.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • pip install -e . works
  • ≥30 pytest cases
  • ASan on .so load test
  • README architecture diagram
Done when: Fresh venv install import and pass tests in 5 minutes.
Q52 MEGA
NumPy Custom Ufunc-Style Operator
Your goal:

Zero-copy numeric bridge.

What you must do:

C++ kernel mutating py::array_t<double> with broadcasting shape checks; expose as Python function; compare NumPy equivalent speed.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Correct strided 1D/2D cases
  • GIL released in compute
  • Tests invalid shape throws
  • Benchmark 1e7 elems
Done when: Matches NumPy result; faster than pure Python.
Q53 MEGA
Python CLI Driving C++ Simulation
Your goal:

Orchestration vs compute split.

What you must do:

C++ engine steps simulation; Python argparse CLI sets params; progress callback to Python; results as NumPy arrays returned.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • GIL rules documented
  • ≥15 integration tests
  • Clean shutdown
  • Type stubs .pyi optional
Done when: CLI runs end-to-end; callbacks fire.
Q54 MEGA
ctypes/cffi vs pybind11 Comparison
Your goal:

Choose right binding technology.

What you must do:

Same C API exported three ways; ergonomics, safety, build complexity, perf table; recommend per use case.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Shared C core
  • ≥600 word analysis
  • Working examples all three
  • Error handling compared
Done when: Decision matrix with measured build times.
Q55 MEGA
Embedded Python Plugin Host
Your goal:

Embed interpreter in C++ application.

What you must do:

C++ app loads plugins from plugins/*.py implementing required functions via pybind11 embed; sandbox import errors.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Plugin discovery
  • Version mismatch handling
  • ≥10 plugins tested
  • No GIL deadlock demo fixed
Done when: Add plugin without recompiling host.
Q56 MEGA
Cross-Language Memory Arena
Your goal:

Shared buffers between Python and C++.

What you must do:

Allocate buffer owned by C++ shared_ptr; expose NumPy view with base object keeping alive; subviews lifetime tests.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • py::keep_alive tests
  • Use-after-free prevented
  • ASan cross-boundary
  • Document ownership diagram
Done when: NumPy array invalid after owner released — tested.
Q57 MEGA
Thread Pool Bridge With GIL Policy
Your goal:

Concurrency + Python interaction.

What you must do:

C++ thread pool executes jobs; Python submits from multiple threads; release GIL during work; callback re-acquires for Python result handler.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • TSan on C++ side
  • Python thread test concurrent submit
  • ≥20 tests
  • Document deadlock pitfalls
Done when: Parallel Python threads safe; results ordered or documented unordered.
Q58 MEGA
Performance Lab Five Kernels
Your goal:

Quantify bridge value.

What you must do:

Five kernels: dot product, matvec, reduce, sort, string concat — Python vs pybind11 vs NumPy; plot; when not to bridge.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • Fair warmup
  • ≥3 sizes each
  • GIL release where applicable
  • Written conclusions
Done when: Clear crossover size where C++ wins.
Q59 MEGA
Custom Type Caster Library
Your goal:

Advanced pybind11 extension points.

What you must do:

Custom casters for Point3, Result<T,E>, optional grid types; non-intrusive; work in STL containers.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥4 custom types
  • Error messages readable
  • ≥18 pytest tests
  • Header reusable
Done when: Types roundtrip Python↔C++ in containers.
Q60 MEGA
Capstone Wrap Prior C++ Library
Your goal:

Integrate curriculum into importable module.

What you must do:

Pick best prior capstone (graph, cache, or sim); full pybind11 bindings; Pythonic API design; packaging; tutorial notebook or script.

Requirements (must all be satisfied):
  • ≥8 public Python functions/classes
  • Prior C++ tests still pass native
  • Extension tests + native tests CI
  • Migration guide Python vs C++ API
Done when: Python user accomplishes task without touching C++.

C++ Mastery Roadmap — After This Track

Phase 1: Core fluency (follow topics 01–13 in order)

  • Weeks 1–2: Compilation, types, pointers — draw memory every day.
  • Weeks 3–4: RAII, classes, Rule of Five — no raw owning pointers.
  • Weeks 5–6: STL containers/algorithms + move semantics.
  • Weeks 7–8: Smart pointers, templates, sanitizers as habit.

Phase 2: Systems & speed (topics 14–18)

  • Concurrency with ThreadSanitizer
  • CMake + Catch2/GoogleTest CI
  • pybind11: expose hot C++ core to Python
  • Profile Python vs C++ extension on one real bottleneck

Folder structure

python/
  python-master.html
  practice/           # Python solutions
  practice_cpp/       # C++ solutions (CMake per topic)
    01_fundamentals/
    ...
    capstones/
  bridge/             # pybind11 projects combining both

Python ↔ C++ Bridge

Use Python for orchestration and C++ for speed — after both tracks' fundamentals. No blockers if Python topics 01–07 and C++ topics 01–13 are solid.

Decision rule: Profile in Python first. If >30% time in one tight loop or memory-bound kernel, extract to C++ with pybind11.

1. pybind11 (recommended)

# CMake + pybind11_add_module
#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
PYBIND11_MODULE(fastmath, m) {
    m.def("add", &add);
}

2. ctypes / C ABI

Export extern "C" functions from shared library; load with ctypes.CDLL. Simpler, no C++ classes across boundary.

3. Practice projects (do in order)

  1. Sum of array: Python baseline → C++ extension → benchmark.
  2. Matrix multiply: NumPy vs raw C++ row-major — discuss cache.
  3. JSON parse stub: Python orchestrates, C++ scans tokens.
  4. MEGA: Image filter pipeline — Python reads PNG, C++ convolves, Python displays.

4. Ownership at the boundary

  • NumPy arrays: py::array_t<double> with explicit shapes.
  • Never return pointer to stack from C++ to Python.
  • GIL: release with py::gil_scoped_release for long CPU work.
